- ☐ **Causal & Explainable AI** — moving from correlation to causation; SHAP, causal forests, do-calculus.
- ☐ **Privacy-Preserving Federated Learning** — FedAvg + Differential Privacy + Secure Aggregation across hospitals.
- ☐ **Generative AI & Digital Twins** — diffusion models for protein/molecule design; patient-specific simulators.

### The 12 Must-Know Formulas
- ☐ $\text{Attn}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}(QK^{\top}/\sqrt{d_k})V$
- ☐ $\text{MHA} = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \dots, \text{head}_h)W^O$
- ☐ $\mathcal{L}_{\text{VAE}} = \mathbb{E}_{q(Z \vert X)}[\log p(X \vert Z)] - \text{KL}(q \Vert \mathcal{N}(0,I))$
- ☐ $w_{t+1} = \sum_{k} (n_k/N)\, w_{t+1}^{(k)}$
- ☐ $\tilde g = g + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2 S^2 I)$
- ☐ $\text{ATE} = \mathbb{E}[Y \vert \text{do}(X{=}1)] - \mathbb{E}[Y \vert \text{do}(X{=}0)]$
- ☐ $x_t = \sqrt{\bar\alpha_t}\,x_0 + \sqrt{1-\bar\alpha_t}\,\epsilon$
- ☐ $F_1 = 2PR/(P+R)$
- ☐ $\text{AUC} = \int_0^1 \text{TPR}(\text{FPR}^{-1}(u))\, du$
- ☐ $\Delta L^{(j)} = L_{\text{perm}(j)} - L_{\text{orig}}$ (Permutation importance)
- ☐ $I(X;Z) = \int p(x,z)\log[p(x,z)/(p(x)p(z))]\,dx\,dz$
- ☐ $w_{t+1} = w_t - \eta\, \hat m_t / (\sqrt{\hat v_t} + \epsilon)$ (Adam)

### Key Terminology to Define if Asked
- ☐ **Self-supervised learning** (predict masked tokens from unlabeled data)
- ☐ **Emergent ability** (zero-shot generalization at scale)
- ☐ **Fine-tuning** (small LR update on downstream task)
- ☐ **Heterogeneity penalty $\zeta$** in FL convergence
- ☐ **Rényi DP accountant** (composes $\epsilon$ across rounds)
- ☐ **DAG** (Directed Acyclic Graph) for causal inference
- ☐ **Counterfactual explanation** ("what if this gene were not mutated?")

### Key Distinctions Examiners Love to Test
- ☐ Supervised vs. self-supervised learning
- ☐ Multi-omics vs. multi-modal
- ☐ FL vs. centralized learning
- ☐ Differential Privacy vs. Secure Aggregation
- ☐ Correlation vs. causation
- ☐ Generative vs. discriminative models
- ☐ Tokenization for proteins (3-mer) vs. DNA (6-mer)

### One-Line Take-Home Message
> *"The future of ML in computational biology is **generalist, multi-modal, causal, privacy-preserving, and generative** — moving from narrow classifiers trained on small labeled datasets to **foundation models** that learn the entire language of life."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Clustering Problems in Computational Biology: Hierarchical Clustering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT633 - MACHINE LEARNING IN COMPUTATIONAL BIOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Clustering Problems in Computational Biology: Hierarchical Clustering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hierarchical Clustering (HC)** is an unsupervised machine learning algorithm that seeks to build a hierarchy of clusters by iteratively merging (agglomerative) or splitting (divisive) data points based on a pairwise dissimilarity measure. In computational biology, hierarchical clustering is the foundational technique used to discover intrinsic groupings in high-dimensional biological data such as gene expression profiles, protein sequences, and evolutionary lineages.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Hierarchical clustering is a deterministic clustering paradigm that does not require the prior specification of the number of clusters ($k$). It produces a multilevel nested grouping of objects, represented visually as a **dendrogram** — a binary tree whose leaf nodes correspond to individual biological samples (e.g., genes, proteins, tissues) and whose internal nodes represent clusters of progressively broader biological similarity.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Family Tree of Genes"

Imagine you are a biologist who has measured the activity level of 1,000 genes across 50 cancer patients. You want to know which genes behave **similarly** (i.e., turn on/off together). Hierarchical clustering acts like building a **family genealogy chart** for these genes:

- **Initially**, every gene is its own separate individual (a leaf on the tree).
- **Step-by-step**, you find the two most "behaviourally similar" genes and tie them together with a knot.
- **Then**, you look for the next-most-similar pair (which could be two single genes, or a knot of two genes and another single gene), and tie them with a higher knot.
- **Eventually**, all genes are connected at the top of the tree in one giant knot.

The height of the knot tells you **how similar** the underlying members are. Genes joined by a low knot are nearly identical in behaviour; genes joined only near the top are very different. Cutting the tree horizontally at any height gives you a discrete set of clusters.

> [!NOTE]
> In computational biology, this "family tree" is called a **dendrogram**, and the act of cutting it at a chosen height is called **cluster cutting** or **flat cluster extraction**.

### 1.3 Why Hierarchical Clustering is Central in Computational Biology

| Biological Domain | Use Case of HC |
|-------------------|----------------|
| **Transcriptomics** | Grouping co-expressed genes from microarray/RNA-seq data |
| **Phylogenetics** | Constructing evolutionary trees of species from DNA/protein sequences |
| **Proteomics** | Identifying protein families with similar structural/functional motifs |
| **Clinical Genomics** | Stratifying patients into molecular subtypes (e.g., breast cancer sub-types) |
| **Metagenomics** | Binning contigs into operational taxonomic units (OTUs) |
| **Single-Cell Biology** | Discovering novel cell types from scRNA-seq counts |

### 1.4 Key Characteristics (High-Yield for KTU)

- **Greedy & Deterministic** — Given a distance matrix and linkage rule, the output is reproducible.
- **No $k$ required upfront** — The number of clusters emerges from cutting the dendrogram.
- **Quadratic time complexity** — $O(n^3)$ in naïve agglomerative form; usable for $n \le$ a few thousand samples (typical for a gene panel or a small cohort of patients).
- **Sensitive to noise and outliers** — A single outlier can perturb the merge order.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Paradigms of Hierarchical Clustering

#### 2.1.1 Agglomerative (Bottom-Up) — *Most Common in Computational Biology*

1. Initialize: each of the $n$ data points forms its own singleton cluster. Total clusters = $n$.
2. Compute the $n \times n$ pairwise distance matrix $D$.
3. **Repeat** until only one cluster remains:
   a. Find the pair of clusters $(C_i, C_j)$ with the smallest inter-cluster distance.
   b. Merge $C_i$ and $C_j$ into a new cluster $C_{ij}$.
   c. Update the distance matrix by removing rows/columns for $C_i$ and $C_j$ and adding $C_{ij}$.
4. Record each merge in a **linkage matrix** $Z \in \mathbb{R}^{(n-1) \times 4}$.

#### 2.1.2 Divisive (Top-Down)

1. Initialize: all $n$ points in a single cluster.
2. At each step, select a cluster and split it into two sub-clusters (often by bisecting $k$-means or by the most disparate pair).
3. Continue until each cluster is a singleton.

> [!NOTE]
> Divisive methods are computationally heavier ($O(2^n)$) and rare in genomics. The KTU syllabus and 95% of bioinformatics pipelines default to **agglomerative HC**.

### 2.2 Distance Metrics — How Biological "Similarity" is Quantified

Let $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^p$ be two biological samples (e.g., expression vectors of $p$ genes).

| Metric | Formula | Biological Meaning / When to Use |
|--------|---------|----------------------------------|
| **Euclidean** | $d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sqrt{\sum_{u=1}^{p}(x_u - y_u)^2}$ | Absolute deviation in expression magnitude; sensitive to scale. |
| **Manhattan (L1)** | $d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sum_{u=1}^{p} \vert x_u - y_u \vert$ | Robust to outliers; used in high-dim sparse scRNA-seq. |
| **Minkowski (L$p$)** | $d = \left(\sum \vert x_u - y_u \vert^p\right)^{1/p}$ | Generalised family encompassing L1 and L2. |
| **Pearson Correlation Distance** | $d = 1 - r$, where $r = \frac{\sum (x_u - \bar{x})(y_u - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum(x_u-\bar{x})^2 \sum(y_u-\bar{y})^2}}$ | Captures **shape similarity** of expression profiles (used in $\approx 70\%$ of microarray studies). |
| **Spearman Rank Correlation** | $d = 1 - \rho$ (Pearson on ranks) | Captures monotonic relationships; robust to non-linearity. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **gene expression** data, **Pearson correlation distance** is the KTU-favoured choice because co-expressed genes may differ in absolute magnitude but share a coordinated up/down pattern across conditions.

### 2.3 Linkage Criteria — How Cluster-to-Cluster Distance is Computed

Given two clusters $A$ and $B$ containing samples $a \in A$ and $b \in B$:

| Linkage | Formula | Tendency (Bias) | Biological Use |
|---------|---------|-----------------|----------------|
| **Single** | $d(A,B) = \min_{a\in A,\,b\in B} d(a,b)$ | Chaining — produces long, straggly clusters | Good for detecting elongated expression gradients |
| **Complete** | $d(A,B) = \max_{a\in A,\,b\in B} d(a,b)$ | Compacts — produces tight, spherical clusters | Default for cancer sub-type discovery |
| **Average (UPGMA)** | $d(A,B) = \frac{1}{\vert A\vert \vert B\vert}\sum_{a,b} d(a,b)$ | Compromise between single and complete | **UPGMA is the basis of phylogenetic trees** |
| **Weighted (WPGMA)** | $d(A,B) = \frac{1}{2}[\,d(A,C) + d(B,C)\,]$ where $C$ is the merged parent | Useful for unequal cluster sizes | Rare in practice |
| **Ward** | $\Delta(A,B) = \frac{\vert A\vert \vert B\vert}{\vert A\vert + \vert B\vert} \Vert \bar{x}_A - \bar{x}_B \Vert^2$ | Minimises within-cluster variance | Preferred for gene/sample matrices in RNA-seq |

> [!NOTE]
> **UPGMA (Unweighted Pair Group Method with Arithmetic Mean)** is the de-facto standard for constructing **phylogenetic dendrograms** in evolutionary biology. It assumes a **molecular clock** — that all lineages diverge at the same rate.

### 2.4 The Linkage Matrix $Z$

The output of `scipy.cluster.hierarchy.linkage` and similar routines is a matrix

$$
Z \in \mathbb{R}^{(n-1) \times 4}
$$

where each row $Z_i = [a_i,\, b_i,\, \delta_i,\, n_i]$ encodes:

- $a_i$ — index of the first cluster merged at step $i$
- $b_i$ — index of the second cluster merged at step $i$
- $\delta_i$ — the distance at which they were merged (height in the dendrogram)
- $n_i$ — number of original observations in the newly formed cluster

> [!TIP]
> In KTU board exams, students are frequently asked to "draw the dendrogram from the linkage matrix" or "interpret the merge heights." Always remember: **lower $\delta_i$ = greater similarity**.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition | Constraint / Range |
|---------------|------------|---------------------|
| $n$ | Number of biological samples | $n \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ |
| $p$ | Number of features (genes/proteins) | $p \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ |
| $D^{(t)}_{ij}$ | Distance between clusters $i,j$ at iteration $t$ | $D \geq 0$, symmetric |
| $r(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y})$ | Pearson correlation coefficient | $-1 \leq r \leq 1$ |
| $c$ | Cophenetic correlation coefficient | $0 \leq c \leq 1$ |
| $\delta_i$ | Merge height at step $i$ | $\delta_1 \leq \delta_2 \leq \dots \leq \delta_{n-1}$ |
| $W(C)$ | Within-cluster inertia of cluster $C$ | $W \geq 0$ |
| $k$ | Number of clusters after cutting the dendrogram | $1 \leq k \leq n$ |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In translational bioinformatics pipelines (e.g., TCGA, GEO, cBioPortal), hierarchical clustering is the **first exploratory step** before any supervised model is built. It reveals whether a disease cohort splits into molecular sub-types (which then become class labels for classification), and whether batch effects are present. The same algorithmic kernel powers **BLAST clustering**, **CD-HIT** for redundancy reduction in sequence databases, and **UCLUST/MOTHUR** for OTU binning in microbiome studies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — Agglomerative HC on Five Genes

**Setup.** Suppose we have the following 1-D expression intensities (already log2-normalised) for five genes across a single condition:

| Gene | $x$ |
|------|-----|
| $g_1$ | 1.0 |
| $g_2$ | 1.5 |
| $g_3$ | 2.0 |
| $g_4$ | 8.0 |
| $g_5$ | 8.5 |

We will use **Euclidean** distance and **single linkage** for clarity.

#### Step 1 — Initial distance matrix $D^{(0)}$

$$
D^{(0)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 0.5 & 1.0 & 7.0 & 7.5 \\
0.5 & 0 & 0.5 & 6.5 & 7.0 \\
1.0 & 0.5 & 0 & 6.0 & 6.5 \\
7.0 & 6.5 & 6.0 & 0 & 0.5 \\
7.5 & 7.0 & 6.5 & 0.5 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

The smallest off-diagonal entry is $0.5$, which appears three times: $(g_1, g_2)$, $(g_2, g_3)$, and $(g_4, g_5)$. We pick the first one (ties broken by row order).

#### Step 2 — First merge

Merge $g_1$ and $g_2$ into cluster $C_{12} = \{g_1, g_2\}$ at height $\delta_1 = 0.5$.

Linkage row:
$$
Z_1 = [1,\, 2,\, 0.5,\, 2]
$$

#### Step 3 — Update distance matrix with single linkage

With single linkage, $d(C_{12}, C_j) = \min\{d(g_1, C_j),\, d(g_2, C_j)\}$.

For $C_{12}$ vs $g_3$: $\min\{1.0,\, 0.5\} = 0.5$.

For $C_{12}$ vs $g_4$: $\min\{7.0,\, 6.5\} = 6.5$.

For $C_{12}$ vs $g_5$: $\min\{7.5,\, 7.0\} = 7.0$.

$$
D^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix}
 & C_{12} & g_3 & g_4 & g_5 \\
C_{12} & 0 & 0.5 & 6.5 & 7.0 \\
g_3 & 0.5 & 0 & 6.0 & 6.5 \\
g_4 & 6.5 & 6.0 & 0 & 0.5 \\
g_5 & 7.0 & 6.5 & 0.5 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

#### Step 4 — Second merge

Smallest entry is again $0.5$ between $C_{12}$ and $g_3$. Merge to $C_{123} = \{g_1, g_2, g_3\}$ at height $\delta_2 = 0.5$.

Linkage row:
$$
Z_2 = [3,\, 4,\, 0.5,\, 3]
$$

(Note: cluster $C_{12}$ was assigned index 3 in the linkage matrix; $g_3$ is index 4 in the original indexing; conventions vary — the student must follow the algorithm's internal labelling.)

#### Step 5 — Update distance matrix

For $C_{123}$ vs $g_4$: $\min\{6.5,\, 6.0\} = 6.0$.

For $C_{123}$ vs $g_5$: $\min\{7.0,\, 6.5\} = 6.5$.

$$
D^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix}
 & C_{123} & g_4 & g_5 \\
C_{123} & 0 & 6.0 & 6.5 \\
g_4 & 6.0 & 0 & 0.5 \\
g_5 & 6.5 & 0.5 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

#### Step 6 — Third merge

Merge $g_4$ and $g_5$ at $\delta_3 = 0.5$:
$$
Z_3 = [4,\, 5,\, 0.5,\, 2]
$$

#### Step 7 — Update distance matrix

For $C_{45}$ vs $C_{123}$: $\min\{6.0,\, 6.5,\, 6.5\} = 6.0$.

$$
D^{(3)} = \begin{bmatrix}
 & C_{123} & C_{45} \\
C_{123} & 0 & 6.0 \\
C_{45} & 6.0 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

#### Step 8 — Final merge

Merge $C_{123}$ and $C_{45}$ at $\delta_4 = 6.0$:
$$
Z_4 = [6,\, 7,\, 6.0,\, 5]
$$

#### Final Linkage Matrix

$$
Z = \begin{bmatrix}
1 & 2 & 0.5 & 2 \\
3 & 4 & 0.5 & 3 \\
4 & 5 & 0.5 & 2 \\
6 & 7 & 6.0 & 5
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### 3.2 Resulting Dendrogram

```
Height
  6.0 ┤         ┌─────────────┐
      │         │             │
      │         │             │
  0.5 ┤    ┌────┴───┐    ┌────┴───┐
      │    │        │    │        │
      │   g1 g2     g3   g4       g5
```

Cutting at height $\approx 1.0$ yields **2 clusters**: $\{g_1, g_2, g_3\}$ (low expression) and $\{g_4, g_5\}$ (high expression). This is biologically meaningful — the first three genes may be a co-regulated low-expression module, the latter two a co-regulated high-expression module.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Cophenetic Correlation Coefficient (Validation Metric)

The **cophenetic distance** $c(i,j)$ is defined as the height $\delta$ at which samples $i$ and $j$ first belong to the same cluster in the dendrogram.

The cophenetic correlation coefficient $c_{\text{coef}}$ is the Pearson correlation between the original distance matrix entries $d(i,j)$ and the cophenetic distances $c(i,j)$:

$$
c_{\text{coef}} = \frac{\sum_{i<j}\bigl(d(i,j) - \bar{d}\bigr)\bigl(c(i,j) - \bar{c}\bigr)}{\sqrt{\sum_{i<j}\bigl(d(i,j) - \bar{d}\bigr)^2 \sum_{i<j}\bigl(c(i,j) - \bar{c}\bigr)^2}}
$$

where

$$
\bar{d} = \frac{1}{\binom{n}{2}}\sum_{i<j} d(i,j), \qquad \bar{c} = \frac{1}{\binom{n}{2}}\sum_{i<j} c(i,j).
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A value $c_{\text{coef}} \geq 0.75$ indicates that the dendrogram faithfully preserves the original pairwise distances — a strong validation of clustering quality. KTU examiners frequently cite $c_{\text{coef}}$ as a quality indicator in viva voce.

### 3.4 Derivation of the Lance–Williams Update Formula

A general formula unifies all linkage criteria. When clusters $A$ and $B$ merge to form $R$, the distance from $R$ to any other cluster $C$ is:

$$
d(R, C) = \alpha_A \, d(A, C) + \alpha_B \, d(B, C) + \beta \, d(A, B) + \gamma \, \vert d(A, C) - d(B, C) \vert
$$

with the following coefficients:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_A &= \frac{\vert A \vert + \vert C \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert + \vert C \vert}, \\[4pt]
\alpha_B &= \frac{\vert B \vert + \vert C \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert + \vert C \vert}, \\[4pt]
\beta &= -\frac{\vert C \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert + \vert C \vert}, \\[4pt]
\gamma &= 0.
\end{aligned}
$$

| Linkage | $\alpha_A$ | $\alpha_B$ | $\beta$ | $\gamma$ |
|---------|-----------|-----------|---------|----------|
| Single | $1/2$ | $1/2$ | $0$ | $-1/2$ |
| Complete | $1/2$ | $1/2$ | $0$ | $+1/2$ |
| Average (UPGMA) | $\frac{\vert A\vert}{\vert A\vert + \vert B\vert}$ | $\frac{\vert B\vert}{\vert A\vert + \vert B\vert}$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| Ward | $\frac{\vert A\vert + \vert C\vert}{\vert A\vert + \vert B\vert + \vert C\vert}$ | $\frac{\vert B\vert + \vert C\vert}{\vert A\vert + \vert B\vert + \vert C\vert}$ | $-\frac{\vert C\vert}{\vert A\vert + \vert B\vert + \vert C\vert}$ | $0$ |

> [!TIP]
> Memorising the Lance–Williams table earns full marks on a 7-mark KTU sub-question asking "Compare linkage methods."

### 3.5 Time and Space Complexity

| Operation | Naïve Agglomerative | Optimised (NN-Chain) |
|-----------|---------------------|----------------------|
| Distance matrix | $O(n^2)$ memory | $O(n^2)$ |
| Per merge | $O(n^2)$ scan | $O(n)$ |
| Total | $O(n^3)$ time | $O(n^2)$ time (for single/complete) |

For $n = 10{,}000$ genes, $O(n^3) = 10^{12}$ operations — infeasible. This is why **practical bioinformatics** uses:
1. **Subsampling** then clustering the representatives.
2. **Approximate methods** like **BIRCH** or **CURE**.
3. **Mini-batch k-means pre-clustering**, followed by HC on the cluster centroids.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Agglomerative HC Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Biological Data Matrix X] --> B[Rows: Genes/Proteins/Tissues]
    A --> C[Columns: Samples/Conditions]
    B --> D[Step 1: Pre-process - Log2 Normalise - Quantile Normalise - Batch Correct]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Step 2: Choose Distance Metric - Euclidean / Pearson / Spearman]
    E --> F[Step 3: Compute Pairwise Distance Matrix D]
    F --> G[Step 4: Choose Linkage - Single / Complete / Average / Ward]
    G --> H[Step 5: Iterative Merging - Find min D_ij - Merge clusters - Update D]
    H --> I[Step 6: Construct Linkage Matrix Z]
    I --> J[Step 7: Render Dendrogram]
    J --> K[Step 8: Validate via Cophenetic Correlation]
    K --> L{Biological Interpretation}
    L --> M[Cut at chosen height - Extract k clusters]
    L --> N[GO Enrichment / Pathway Analysis]
    L --> O[Survival Analysis per Cluster]
```

### 4.2 Linkage Method Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Biological Question] --> Q1{Goal?}
    Q1 -->|Phylogenetic tree| P1[UPGMA - Average Linkage]
    Q1 -->|Co-expression modules| P2[Ward Linkage]
    Q1 -->|Cell-type discovery| P3[Complete Linkage]
    Q1 -->|Elongated gradients| P4[Single Linkage]
    P1 --> Check[Validate with Cophenetic Correlation >= 0.75]
    P2 --> Check
    P3 --> Check
    P4 --> Check
    Check --> Result[Robust Cluster Assignments]
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Architecture — Hierarchical Clustering in a Bioinformatics Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DataLayer[Data Acquisition Layer]
        GEO[GEO Database]
        TCGA[TCGA Portal]
        SRA[Sequence Read Archive]
    end

    subgraph PreProc[Pre-Processing Layer]
        Norm[Normalisation]
        Batch[Batch Correction]
        Filter[Low-Variance Filtering]
    end

    subgraph ClusterEng[Clustering Engine]
        DM[Distance Matrix Builder]
        LM[Linkage Engine]
        DD[Dendrogram Generator]
    end

    subgraph Validation[Validation Layer]
        Coph[Cophenetic Correlation]
        Boot[Bootstrap Resampling]
        Sil[Silhouette Score]
    end

    subgraph Downstream[Downstream Biology]
        GO[GO / KEGG Enrichment]
        Surv[Kaplan-Meier Survival]
        Net[Gene Co-expression Networks]
    end

    GEO --> Norm
    TCGA --> Norm
    SRA --> Norm
    Norm --> Batch --> Filter --> DM --> LM --> DD
    DD --> Coph
    DD --> Boot
    DD --> Sil
    Coph --> Downstream
    Boot --> Downstream
    Sil --> Downstream
    DD --> Downstream
```

### 4.4 Dendrogram Anatomy — Annotated Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[g1] --- L2[g2] --- L3[g3] --- L4[g4] --- L5[g5]
    M1(( )) --- M2(( ))
    N1[ ] --- N2[ ]
    H1[ ] --- H2[ ]
    Top((Root Cluster))
    L1 -.- M1
    L2 -.- M1
    L3 -.- M2
    L4 -.- M2
    L5 -.- M2
    M1 --- N1
    M2 --- N2
    N1 --- H1
    N2 --- H2
    H1 --- Top
    H2 --- Top
```

> Where: leaves $L_i$ are individual samples, $M_i$ are first-level merges, $N_i$ are intermediate clusters, $H_i$ are higher-level merges, and the **Root** is the single cluster containing all samples. Vertical position encodes the **merge height $\delta$**.

### 4.5 Comparison Block — Hierarchical vs Partitional Clustering

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Clustering Algorithms] --> B[Hierarchical]
    A --> C[Partitional]
    B --> B1[Agglomerative HC]
    B --> B2[Divisive HC]
    C --> C1[K-Means]
    C --> C2[K-Medoids]
    C --> C3[DBSCAN]
    B1 --> D[Output: Dendrogram - No k required - O n^3]
    C1 --> E[Output: Flat Labels - k required - O nkt]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define hierarchical clustering. Differentiate between agglomerative and divisive clustering with a suitable biological example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Hierarchical clustering is an unsupervised learning technique that builds a nested tree of clusters (dendrogram) by iteratively merging or splitting data points based on pairwise dissimilarity. In computational biology, it is used to cluster co-expressed genes from microarray data.

- **Agglomerative (Bottom-Up):** Each sample begins as a singleton cluster; the two closest clusters are merged at each step until one cluster remains. **Biological example:** Constructing a phylogenetic tree of species from DNA sequence distances.
- **Divisive (Top-Down):** All samples begin in one cluster; the most heterogeneous cluster is split at each step until each sample is isolated. **Biological example:** Rarely used; one example is recursively partitioning a tissue sample hierarchy.

> [!NOTE]
> Award 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for distinguishing the two paradigms, and 1 mark for the biological example.

---

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List and briefly explain any three linkage methods used in agglomerative hierarchical clustering.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Single Linkage:** Distance between two clusters = minimum distance between any pair of points. Tendency to form chaining (long, straggly clusters). Used to detect elongated co-expression gradients.
2. **Complete Linkage:** Distance = maximum distance between any pair of points. Produces compact, spherical clusters. Widely used in cancer sub-typing.
3. **Average Linkage (UPGMA):** Distance = mean of all pairwise distances. Balances single and complete. The basis of phylogenetic tree construction under the molecular-clock assumption.
4. **Ward's Method:** Merges clusters that minimise the increase in total within-cluster variance. Preferred for high-dimensional RNA-seq data.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A `[KTU University Exam – July 2024]`

**(a)** Describe the agglomerative hierarchical clustering algorithm with a suitable block diagram. Discuss the role of the distance metric and linkage criterion. State the time and space complexity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the following Euclidean distance matrix for five gene-expression profiles $g_1, g_2, g_3, g_4, g_5$, perform agglomerative clustering using **complete linkage** and construct the dendrogram. **(7 marks)**

$$
D = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 2 & 6 & 10 & 9 \\
2 & 0 & 5 & 9 & 8 \\
6 & 5 & 0 & 4 & 3 \\
10 & 9 & 4 & 0 & 2 \\
9 & 8 & 3 & 2 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Algorithm Walkthrough (7 marks)**

1. **Input** (1 mark): A data matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p}$ of $n$ biological samples over $p$ features (genes/proteins).
2. **Step 1** (1 mark): Standardise $X$ (e.g., z-score per gene) to remove magnitude bias.
3. **Step 2** (1 mark): Compute the $n \times n$ pairwise distance matrix $D$ using a chosen metric (Euclidean for absolute expression, Pearson correlation for shape similarity).
4. **Step 3** (1 mark): At each iteration $t = 1, 2, \dots, n-1$:
   - Identify the pair $(C_i, C_j)$ minimising $D_{ij}$ under the chosen **linkage** rule.
   - Merge $C_i$ and $C_j$ into $C_{ij}$.
   - Update $D$ using the **Lance–Williams recurrence**.
5. **Step 4** (1 mark): Record each merge in the linkage matrix $Z$ and render the dendrogram.
6. **Complexity** (1 mark): $O(n^2)$ memory for $D$, $O(n^3)$ time naïvely, $O(n^2)$ for nearest-neighbour-chain optimisation.
7. **Block diagram** (1 mark): The Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes covers this.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Iteration 1:** Smallest entry in $D$ is $D_{12} = 2$ and $D_{45} = 2$. Pick $(g_4, g_5)$ first (alphabetical convention); merge at $\delta_1 = 2$.

**Update with complete linkage:** $d(C_{45}, C_i) = \max\{d(g_4, C_i),\, d(g_5, C_i)\}$.

For $C_{45}$ vs $g_1$: $\max\{10, 9\} = 10$.

For $C_{45}$ vs $g_2$: $\max\{9, 8\} = 9$.

For $C_{45}$ vs $g_3$: $\max\{4, 3\} = 4$.

**Iteration 2:** New distances: $D_{12} = 2$ remains smallest. Merge $(g_1, g_2)$ at $\delta_2 = 2$.

For $C_{12}$ vs $g_3$: $\max\{6, 5\} = 6$.

For $C_{12}$ vs $C_{45}$: $\max\{10, 9\} = 10$.

For $C_{12}$ vs $g_3$: 6.

For $C_{12}$ vs $C_{45}$: 10.

**Iteration 3:** Current matrix (3 clusters): $\{C_{12}\}, \{g_3\}, \{C_{45}\}$. Distances: $d(C_{12}, g_3) = 6$, $d(g_3, C_{45}) = 4$, $d(C_{12}, C_{45}) = 10$. Merge $(g_3, C_{45})$ at $\delta_3 = 4$ to form $C_{345}$.

**Iteration 4:** Distances: $d(C_{12}, C_{345}) = \max\{6, 4\} = 6$ (complete linkage uses max). Wait — recompute: $d(C_{12}, C_{345}) = \max\{d(C_{12}, g_3), d(C_{12}, g_4), d(C_{12}, g_5)\} = \max\{6, 10, 9\} = 10$. Merge $C_{12}$ and $C_{345}$ at $\delta_4 = 10$.

**Linkage matrix:**

$$
Z = \begin{bmatrix}
4 & 5 & 2 & 2 \\
1 & 2 & 2 & 2 \\
3 & 6 & 4 & 3 \\
7 & 8 & 10 & 5
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Dendrogram:**

```
Height
 10.0 ┤       ┌───────────────┐
      │       │               │
      │       │               │
   4.0 ┤       │         ┌─────┴────┐
      │       │         │          │
   2.0 ┤    ┌──┴──┐   g3        ┌──┴──┐
      │    │     │              │     │
      │   g1    g2             g4    g5
```

**Biological interpretation:** Genes $g_4$ and $g_5$ are the most tightly co-regulated pair (merge at height 2). Genes $g_1$ and $g_2$ form a second tight pair. The two pairs are maximally different from each other (final merge at height 10), indicating two distinct co-expression modules.

---

#### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of the **cophenetic correlation coefficient** as a validation metric for hierarchical clustering. Derive its mathematical formula. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the application of hierarchical clustering in **gene expression analysis**. With a neat sketch, explain the steps involved in constructing a gene-expression heatmap with an attached dendrogram. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Cophenetic Correlation (7 marks)**

**Definition (2 marks):** The cophenetic distance $c(i,j)$ between two samples $i$ and $j$ is the height $\delta$ at which they first join the same cluster in the dendrogram. The cophenetic correlation coefficient is the Pearson correlation between the original pairwise distances $d(i,j)$ and the cophenetic distances $c(i,j)$ for all $\binom{n}{2}$ pairs.

**Derivation (3 marks):** Let $\bar{d}$ and $\bar{c}$ be the means of the off-diagonal entries of $D$ and $C$ respectively.

$$
c_{\text{coef}} = \frac{\sum_{i<j}(d_{ij} - \bar{d})(c_{ij} - \bar{c})}{\sqrt{\sum_{i<j}(d_{ij} - \bar{d})^2 \cdot \sum_{i<j}(c_{ij} - \bar{c})^2}}
$$

This measures the degree to which the dendrogram preserves the original pairwise structure. Values close to **1.0** indicate high fidelity; values below **0.7** suggest that the chosen linkage/metric combination is distorting the data structure.

**Validation use (2 marks):** One can sweep over (metric, linkage) pairs and pick the combination that maximises $c_{\text{coef}}$. This is a standard model-selection criterion in computational biology pipelines (e.g., in the R package `pheatmap` and the Python `scipy.cluster.hierarchy.cophenet`).

**Part (b) — Gene-Expression Heatmap with Dendrogram (7 marks)**

1. **Input** (1 mark): A gene-expression matrix $G \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p}$ with $n$ genes (rows) and $p$ samples (columns), log2-transformed and quantile-normalised.
2. **Filter** (1 mark): Remove low-variance and low-expression genes (e.g., keep top 2,000 by variance).
3. **Z-score per gene** (1 mark): Each row is standardised so up-regulation and down-regulation are comparable in the colour scale.
4. **Cluster rows (genes)** (1 mark): Apply HC with Pearson distance + average linkage → gene dendrogram on the left.
5. **Cluster columns (samples)** (1 mark): Apply HC with Euclidean distance + Ward linkage → sample dendrogram on the top.
6. **Render** (1 mark): Display the Z-score matrix as a heatmap (red = up, blue = down) with both dendrograms attached. Cut the sample dendrogram to reveal disease sub-types.
7. **Annotate** (1 mark): Add colour bars for clinical covariates (tumour vs normal, mutation status).

**Sketch:**

```
            ┌─────────────┐
            │  Tumour A   │
            ├──────┬──────┤
            │ TuB  │  TuC │
            └──┬───┴──┬───┘
   ┌───────────┴──┐ ┌─┴─────────────┐
   │ Module 1     │ │ Module 2       │
   │  (up in TuB) │ │ (down in TuA)  │
   │  g1,g2,g3    │ │ g10,g11,g12    │
   └──────────────┘ └────────────────┘
   [Heatmap with red/blue gradient and row + col dendrograms]
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to update the distance matrix** after each merge. (Loses 1–2 marks.)
> 2. **Confusing single vs complete linkage formulas.** Single uses **min**; complete uses **max**. Mixing them up is the #1 error.
> 3. **Not stating the time complexity** in algorithmic questions. KTU almost always awards 1 mark for complexity.
> 4. **Using Euclidean distance for gene expression** without justification. Pearson is preferred when expression *shape* matters more than absolute magnitude.
> 5. **Skipping the biological interpretation** of the dendrogram. KTU values the translational biology commentary as much as the algorithm steps.
> 6. **Forgetting that Ward's method requires Euclidean distance** — it is mathematically undefined under correlation distance.
> 7. **Misinterpreting the linkage matrix** $Z$ columns. The third column is the **merge height** $\delta$, not a probability.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Hierarchical clustering = dendrogram.** No need to pre-specify $k$.
- **Agglomerative (bottom-up) is the default** in computational biology; divisive is rare.
- **Distance metric choice** is biology-driven: Pearson for co-expression *shape*; Euclidean for *magnitude*; Spearman for monotonic rank-preserving relationships.
- **Linkage choice** is method-driven: single → chains; complete → compact; average (UPGMA) → phylogenetic; Ward → minimum variance.
- **Lance–Williams update** unifies all linkage criteria in one recurrence — a frequently-tested KTU point.
- **Cophenetic correlation $c_{\text{coef}} \geq 0.75$** is the gold-standard validation heuristic.
- **Complexity** is $O(n^3)$ naïvely; $O(n^2)$ with NN-chain. Practical $n \lesssim 10{,}000$.
- **Applications** span transcriptomics (microarray, RNA-seq), phylogenetics, proteomics, single-cell biology, metagenomics, and clinical sub-typing.
- **Ward linkage requires Euclidean distance** — a strict requirement often overlooked.
- **Dendrogram cutting height** is biologically motivated: choose $\delta$ where the gap in merge heights is largest (the "elbow" rule).
- **Stability** is assessed by **bootstrap resampling** of the samples and recomputing the dendrogram; high cluster co-occurrence across resamples indicates robust clusters.
- **Output interpretation** must always end with biological meaning: GO enrichment, pathway analysis, survival curves, or network reconstruction.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Partition Clustering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT633 - MACHINE LEARNING IN COMPUTATIONAL BIOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Partition Clustering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Partition Clustering

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Partition Clustering** is a category of unsupervised learning algorithms that organizes a dataset of **n** observations into a pre-specified number **k** of non-overlapping, exhaustive subsets (clusters), such that every data point is assigned to **exactly one** cluster based on a defined similarity or dissimilarity criterion. Formally, given a dataset

$$X = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}, \quad x_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$$

partition clustering produces a partition

$$\mathcal{C} = \{C_1, C_2, \dots, C_k\}$$

satisfying the following three axioms:

$$
\bigcup_{j=1}^{k} C_j = X, \quad C_i \cap C_j = \emptyset \;\;(i \neq j), \quad C_j \neq \emptyset
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hard Partitioning Property:** In classical partition clustering, every datum belongs to *one and only one* cluster. This is the principal distinction from **hierarchical clustering** (which produces a tree of nested clusters) and from **soft/fuzzy clustering** (e.g., Fuzzy C-Means, where a point can have graded memberships across multiple clusters).

The most canonical examples are **K-Means** (Lloyd's algorithm, 1957/1982) and **K-Medoids** (Kaufman & Rousseeuw, 1987 — PAM algorithm).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are a **taxonomist in a biodiversity lab** with 500 unlabeled microscope images of cells. You have **three** empty Petri dishes and need to group the cells into 3 morphological categories. Your strategy:

1. **Drop 3 pins** randomly onto your sample tray — these are your initial "**centroids**" (representative centers).
2. **Measure the morphological distance** of every cell to each pin (size, shape, staining intensity).
3. **Assign each cell** to the dish of its nearest pin.
4. **Recompute** the centroid (the new "average cell") of each dish.
5. **Reassign** cells if their nearest pin has changed.
6. **Stop** when no cell changes its dish anymore.

That iterative pin-moving process *is* exactly K-Means. **Partition clustering = divide the population into k piles, then iteratively refine the pile-cores until stable.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters in Computational Biology:**
> - Clustering **gene expression profiles** across thousands of genes (e.g., identifying co-expressed gene modules).
> - Grouping **single-cell RNA-seq (scRNA-seq)** data into putative cell types.
> - **Protein family** discovery from sequence/structure features.
> - **Patient stratification** based on multi-omics biomarkers.

### 1.3 Key Entities in Partition Clustering (Bolded Glossary)

- **Cluster centroid** — the mean (or medoid) of points belonging to a cluster.
- **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)** — the total intra-cluster variance; the objective K-Means minimizes.
- **Distance metric** — typically Euclidean (L2), but Manhattan (L1), cosine, or Mahalanobis distances are also valid.
- **Convergence criterion** — typically the change in WCSS falling below a tolerance **ε**, or a maximum iteration count.
- **k** — the user-specified number of clusters (a hyperparameter).
- **Initialization strategy** — Forgy, Random Partition, or **K-Means++** (Arthur & Vassilvitskii, 2007).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The K-Means Algorithm — Operational Breakdown

K-Means is the prototypical partition clustering method. It is an **iterative-refinement / expectation-maximization-style** algorithm that alternates between two steps until convergence.

**Step 0 — Initialization.**
Choose **k** initial centroids $\{\mu_1^{(0)}, \mu_2^{(0)}, \dots, \mu_k^{(0)}\}$ from the dataset (Forgy method) or via **K-Means++ seeding**.

**Step 1 — Assignment (E-step analogue).**
Assign each data point to the cluster whose centroid is nearest (under Euclidean distance):

$$C_j^{(t)} = \left\{ x_i \in X : j = \arg\min_{r \in \{1,\dots,k\}} \Vert x_i - \mu_r^{(t)} \Vert^2 \right\}$$

**Step 2 — Update (M-step analogue).**
Recompute each centroid as the mean of the points currently assigned to it:

$$\mu_j^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{\vert C_j^{(t)} \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_j^{(t)}} x_i$$

**Step 3 — Convergence check.**
Terminate when

$$\vert J^{(t+1)} - J^{(t)} \vert \le \varepsilon$$

where **J** is the WCSS objective. Otherwise, set $t \leftarrow t+1$ and return to Step 1.

**Why these two steps converge:**
The assignment step never increases the objective **J** (each point is moved to the closest centroid in L2 sense). The update step computes the *unique* point minimizer of $\sum_{x \in C_j} \Vert x - \mu \Vert^2$, which is the arithmetic mean. Therefore **J is monotonically non-increasing** and bounded below by 0, guaranteeing convergence to a (local) minimum.

### 2.2 The K-Means Objective Function (WCSS)

The objective K-Means minimizes is the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares**:

$$J(\mathcal{C}, \mu) = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \sum_{x_i \in C_j} \Vert x_i - \mu_j \Vert^2$$

Expanding for Euclidean distance, using $d$ as feature dimensionality:

$$J = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \sum_{i:\,x_i \in C_j} \sum_{p=1}^{d} (x_{ip} - \mu_{jp})^2$$

This is the **trace of the within-cluster scatter matrix** $S_W$:

$$J = \text{tr}(S_W) = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \sum_{x_i \in C_j} (x_i - \mu_j)(x_i - \mu_j)^{\top}$$

Equivalently, the **total scatter identity** links between-cluster and within-cluster variance:

$$\underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \Vert x_i - \bar{x} \Vert^2}_{S_T} \;=\; \underbrace{\sum_{j=1}^{k} \vert C_j \vert \cdot \Vert \mu_j - \bar{x} \Vert^2}_{S_B} \;+\; \underbrace{\sum_{j=1}^{k}\sum_{x_i \in C_j} \Vert x_i - \mu_j \Vert^2}_{S_W}$$

Minimizing $S_W$ is **equivalent** to maximizing the between-cluster scatter $S_B$ (since $S_T$ is constant w.r.t. cluster assignment).

### 2.3 K-Means++ — Smarter Initialization

Instead of random seeding, K-Means++ picks the first centroid uniformly at random, then each subsequent centroid $x_i$ is chosen with probability proportional to its squared distance from the nearest already-chosen centroid:

$$P(x_i \; \text{is chosen}) = \frac{D(x_i)^2}{\sum_{r=1}^{n} D(x_r)^2}, \quad D(x_i) = \min_{m \in M} \Vert x_i - \mu_m \Vert$$

This yields an **O(log k)**-competitive expected solution relative to the global optimum (Arthur & Vassilvitskii, 2007).

### 2.4 K-Medoids (PAM — Partitioning Around Medoids)

K-Means is sensitive to **outliers** because the mean is not a robust estimator. **K-Medoids** replaces the centroid with a **medoid** — the actual data point that minimizes the average dissimilarity to all other points in the cluster.

**Objective (PAM):**

$$J_{\text{PAM}} = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \sum_{x_i \in C_j} d(x_i, m_j)$$

where $m_j \in C_j$ is the medoid of cluster $j$, and $d(\cdot,\cdot)$ is *any* dissimilarity (Euclidean, Manhattan, or even non-metric such as edit distance for biological sequences).

**PAM update rule:**

$$m_j^{\ast} = \arg\min_{x \in C_j} \sum_{x' \in C_j} d(x, x')$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Variants of K-Medoids useful in Computational Biology:**
> - **CLARA** (Clustering LARge Applications) — samples subsets and applies PAM to each.
> - **CLARANS** — randomized search over the medoid configuration space; more scalable.

### 2.5 Fuzzy C-Means (Soft Partitioning) — Brief Note

Each datum has a **membership grade** $u_{ij} \in [0,1]$ summing to 1 across clusters. Objective:

$$J_{FCM} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{k} u_{ij}^{m} \Vert x_i - v_j \Vert^2, \quad m > 1 \text{ (fuzzifier)}$$

Update equations:

$$u_{ij} = \left[ \sum_{r=1}^{k} \left( \frac{\Vert x_i - v_j \Vert}{\Vert x_i - v_r \Vert} \right)^{\frac{2}{m-1}} \right]^{-1}, \quad v_j = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} u_{ij}^{m} x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} u_{ij}^{m}}$$

Useful in gene-expression data when cluster boundaries are genuinely ambiguous.

### 2.6 Choosing k — the Elbow & Silhouette Methods

Two practical diagnostics KTU examiners love to test:

**Elbow method:** Plot $J(k)$ (WCSS) versus $k$ and look for the "elbow" — the value of $k$ where marginal reduction in WCSS begins to plateau.

**Silhouette coefficient** for point $i$:

$$s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$$

where $a(i)$ = mean intra-cluster distance, and $b(i)$ = mean distance to the *nearest* neighboring cluster. Range: $[-1, +1]$. Higher is better.

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|---------------|
| 1 | K-Means objective (WCSS) | $J = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \sum_{x_i \in C_j} \Vert x_i - \mu_j \Vert^2$ | Sum of squared L2 distances |
| 2 | Centroid update | $\mu_j = \frac{1}{\vert C_j \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_j} x_i$ | Vector of dimension $d$ |
| 3 | Cluster assignment | $C_j = \{x_i : \arg\min_r \Vert x_i - \mu_r \Vert^2\}$ | Argmin over k centroids |
| 4 | Total scatter identity | $S_T = S_B + S_W$ | Constant minus within equals between |
| 5 | K-Means++ probability | $P(x_i) \propto D(x_i)^2$ | Squared distance from nearest seed |
| 6 | K-Medoids (PAM) objective | $J_{PAM} = \sum_{j} \sum_{x_i \in C_j} d(x_i, m_j)$ | Robust to outliers |
| 7 | Silhouette score | $s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$ | Range $[-1, +1]$ |
| 8 | FCM membership | $u_{ij} = [\sum_r (\Vert x_i - v_j \Vert / \Vert x_i - v_r \Vert)^{\frac{2}{m-1}}]^{-1}$ | Soft partition; $m > 1$ |
| 9 | Convergence criterion | $\vert J^{(t+1)} - J^{(t)} \vert \le \varepsilon$ | Tolerance $\varepsilon$ or max iterations $T_{max}$ |
| 10 | Elbow criterion | Locate kink in $J$ vs $k$ plot | Visual heuristic |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility Beyond Biology:** K-Means underlies vector quantization in signal processing, image compression (color quantization), customer segmentation in marketing, document clustering in NLP, and anomaly detection (smallest-cluster-or-farthest-from-centroid rule). In bioinformatics, it powers tools like **IsoLasso**, **SC3** (single-cell consensus clustering), and **K-means clustering of k-mer frequencies** for sequence binning in metagenomics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Why the Centroid Update Is the Arithmetic Mean

**Claim:** Given a fixed cluster assignment $C_j$, the value of $\mu_j$ that minimizes

$$f(\mu_j) = \sum_{x_i \in C_j} \Vert x_i - \mu_j \Vert^2$$

is the arithmetic mean $\bar{x}_j = \frac{1}{\vert C_j \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_j} x_i$.

**Derivation:**

Set the gradient w.r.t. $\mu_j$ to zero. The objective expands to:

$$f(\mu_j) = \sum_{i \in C_j} (x_i - \mu_j)^{\top}(x_i - \mu_j) = \sum_{i \in C_j} \left( x_i^{\top} x_i - 2 x_i^{\top} \mu_j + \mu_j^{\top} \mu_j \right)$$

Take the gradient (using standard matrix calculus $\nabla_\mu (\mu^{\top}\mu) = 2\mu$ and $\nabla_\mu (a^{\top}\mu) = a$):

$$\nabla_{\mu_j} f(\mu_j) = \sum_{i \in C_j} \left( -2 x_i + 2 \mu_j \right) = 2 \left( \vert C_j \vert \, \mu_j - \sum_{i \in C_j} x_i \right)$$

Set $\nabla_{\mu_j} f(\mu_j) = 0$:

$$\vert C_j \vert \, \mu_j^{\ast} = \sum_{i \in C_j} x_i \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \mu_j^{\ast} = \frac{1}{\vert C_j \vert} \sum_{i \in C_j} x_i \quad \blacksquare$$

**Why this matters:** This is the *rigorous* justification of the M-step of K-Means. KTU examiners frequently ask "show that the optimal centroid is the mean" — this is the standard derivation.

### 3.2 Derivation — Total Scatter Identity

**Claim:** $S_T = S_B + S_W$.

**Derivation:** Let $\bar{x}$ be the global mean. Then for any point $x_i \in C_j$:

$$x_i - \bar{x} = (x_i - \mu_j) + (\mu_j - \bar{x})$$

Therefore:

$$\Vert x_i - \bar{x} \Vert^2 = \Vert x_i - \mu_j \Vert^2 + \Vert \mu_j - \bar{x} \Vert^2 + 2 (x_i - \mu_j)^{\top}(\mu_j - \bar{x})$$

Sum over all points $x_i$ in $C_j$:

$$\sum_{i \in C_j} \Vert x_i - \bar{x} \Vert^2 = \sum_{i \in C_j} \Vert x_i - \mu_j \Vert^2 + \vert C_j \vert \cdot \Vert \mu_j - \bar{x} \Vert^2 + 2 \underbrace{\left(\sum_{i \in C_j}(x_i - \mu_j)\right)^{\top}}_{=\,0}(\mu_j - \bar{x})$$

The cross term vanishes because $\sum_{i \in C_j} (x_i - \mu_j) = 0$ by definition of centroid. Summing over all $k$ clusters:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \Vert x_i - \bar{x} \Vert^2 = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \sum_{i \in C_j} \Vert x_i - \mu_j \Vert^2 + \sum_{j=1}^{k} \vert C_j \vert \cdot \Vert \mu_j - \bar{x} \Vert^2$$

i.e., $S_T = S_W + S_B$. $\blacksquare$

**Consequence:** Since $S_T$ is independent of the partition, **minimizing $S_W$ $\iff$ maximizing $S_B$**. This is the bridge to *Fisher's discriminant* intuition in supervised learning.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — K-Means on a 2-D Mini-Dataset (Hand-Traceable)

**Dataset:** $X = \{(1,1), (1,2), (2,1), (8,8), (9,9), (9,8)\}$, $k=2$.

**Iteration 0 — Initialization (Forgy).** Randomly pick $\mu_1^{(0)} = (1,1)$ and $\mu_2^{(0)} = (8,8)$.

**Iteration 0 — Assignment step.** Compute squared Euclidean distances:

| Point | $\Vert x - \mu_1 \Vert^2$ | $\Vert x - \mu_2 \Vert^2$ | Assigned cluster |
|-------|--------------------------|--------------------------|------------------|
| (1,1) | 0 | 98 | 1 |
| (1,2) | 1 | 85 | 1 |
| (2,1) | 1 | 85 | 1 |
| (8,8) | 98 | 0 | 2 |
| (9,9) | 128 | 2 | 2 |
| (9,8) | 113 | 1 | 2 |

**Update step.** New centroids:
- $\mu_1^{(1)} = \frac{1}{3}\big((1,1)+(1,2)+(2,1)\big) = (4/3,\, 4/3)$
- $\mu_2^{(1)} = \frac{1}{3}\big((8,8)+(9,9)+(9,8)\big) = (26/3,\, 25/3) \approx (8.67,\, 8.33)$

**Compute WCSS $J^{(1)}$:**
$J^{(1)} = (1-4/3)^2+(1-4/3)^2 + (1-4/3)^2+(2-4/3)^2 + (2-4/3)^2+(1-4/3)^2 + (8-26/3)^2+(8-25/3)^2 + (9-26/3)^2+(9-25/3)^2 + (9-26/3)^2+(8-25/3)^2$
$= 3 \cdot 2/9 + 2 \cdot 1/9 + 2 \cdot 4/9 + 2 \cdot 1/9 + 2 \cdot 1/9$
$= 6/9 + 2/9 + 8/9 + 2/9 + 2/9 = 20/9 \approx 2.22$

**Iteration 1 — Re-assignment** (repeating with the new centroids, the assignment remains the same — no flips).

**Convergence:** Assignments are stable, so K-Means has converged after **2 iterations**. Final WCSS = **20/9 ≈ 2.22**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of optimality:** The true underlying cluster means are exactly (4/3, 4/3) and (26/3, 25/3), confirming the algorithm recovered the global optimum because the initial seeds were well-placed. With poor initialization (e.g., both seeds in the same true cluster), K-Means can stall in a *local* minimum — this is why K-Means++ is the default in scikit-learn.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — K-Means vs. K-Medoids vs. Fuzzy C-Means

| Property | K-Means | K-Medoids (PAM) | Fuzzy C-Means |
|----------|---------|-----------------|----------------|
| Cluster representative | Mean (synthetic point) | Medoid (actual data point) | Weighted mean (synthetic) |
| Distance metric | Squared Euclidean (mandatory) | Any dissimilarity | Squared Euclidean (mandatory) |
| Robustness to outliers | **Low** (mean is not robust) | **High** (medoid is robust) | Low |
| Computational cost per iter. | $O(nkd)$ | $O(k(n-k)^2 d)$ — slower | $O(nkd)$ |
| Scalability (large n) | High | Limited; use CLARA/CLARANS | High |
| Membership type | Hard (binary) | Hard (binary) | **Soft (graded)** |
| Best use in bioinformatics | scRNA-seq, expression vectors | Sequence/dissimilarity matrices | Ambiguous cluster boundaries |
| Convergence guarantee | Yes (local min) | Yes (local min) | Yes (local min) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 K-Means Algorithm — Control-Flow Schematic (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: dataset X, k, epsilon] --> B[Initialize k centroids via K-Means plus plus]
    B --> C[Set t = 0]
    C --> D[Assignment step: assign each xi to nearest centroid]
    D --> E[Update step: recompute mu_j as mean of C_j]
    E --> F[Compute WCSS J at iteration t]
    F --> G{Change in J less than epsilon?}
    G -- No --> H[t = t plus 1] --> D
    G -- Yes --> I[Output: final clusters and centroids]
    style A fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#1F4E79,stroke-width:1px
    style I fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#82B366,stroke-width:1px
    style G fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#D6B656,stroke-width:1px
```

### 4.2 Partition Clustering — End-to-End Pipeline (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw biological data: sequences / expression / omics] --> B[Preprocessing: normalization, scaling, log transform]
    B --> C[Feature extraction: k-mer freq, PCA, embeddings]
    C --> D[Distance matrix D: Euclidean, cosine, edit]
    D --> E{Choose algorithm}
    E -- k large, low noise --> F[K-Means]
    E -- outliers, non-Euclidean --> G[K-Medoids PAM]
    E -- soft boundaries --> H[Fuzzy C-Means]
    F --> I[Cluster validation: silhouette, elbow, gap]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Biological interpretation: GO terms, pathway enrichment]
    style A fill:#F8CECC,stroke:#B85450
    style J fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#82B366
    style I fill:#FFE6CC,stroke:#D79B00
```

    P1[Raw count matrix: genes x cells] --> P2[Library size normalization]
    P2 --> P3[Log1p transform]

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 3 marks]**
*Define partition clustering. State the key differences between K-Means and K-Medoids algorithms.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Partition clustering is a class of unsupervised learning algorithms that divides a dataset of $n$ observations into $k$ non-overlapping, exhaustive subsets (clusters), such that each data point is assigned to exactly one cluster based on a similarity criterion. *(1 mark)*

**K-Means** uses the **arithmetic mean** of the points in a cluster as its centroid, minimizes the **WCSS** objective $\sum_j \sum_{x_i \in C_j} \Vert x_i - \mu_j \Vert^2$, requires squared Euclidean distance, and is **sensitive to outliers** because the mean is not robust. *(1 mark)*

**K-Medoids (PAM)** uses an **actual data point** (the medoid) — the point that minimizes total dissimilarity to others in its cluster — as the cluster representative, can use **any dissimilarity metric** (including non-metric ones such as edit distance for biological sequences), and is **robust to outliers**. *(1 mark)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 3 marks]**
*Explain the K-Means++ initialization strategy. Why is it preferred over random initialization?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In **K-Means++**, the first centroid is chosen uniformly at random from the dataset. Each subsequent centroid $x_i$ is chosen with probability proportional to $D(x_i)^2$, where $D(x_i)$ is the distance from $x_i$ to the nearest already-selected centroid. *(2 marks)*

This seeding is preferred because it ensures that the initial centroids are **well-spread** across the data, reducing the chance of converging to a poor local minimum. Arthur & Vassilvitskii (2007) proved that K-Means++ produces an expected objective value that is **O(log k)**-competitive with the global optimum, while pure random seeding can produce arbitrarily bad local minima on adversarial inputs. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **INTERNAL CHOICE — Answer ANY ONE full question (A or B). Each sub-part carries 7 marks.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Question]*

**(a)** Derive the centroid update rule of the K-Means algorithm. Show that the centroid that minimizes the within-cluster sum of squares for a fixed cluster assignment is the arithmetic mean of the points in the cluster. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the K-Means algorithm with the WCSS objective function. Mention the role of K-Means++ initialization. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

For a fixed cluster assignment $C_j$, the within-cluster objective is:

$$f(\mu_j) = \sum_{x_i \in C_j} \Vert x_i - \mu_j \Vert^2 = \sum_{i \in C_j} (x_i - \mu_j)^{\top}(x_i - \mu_j)$$

Expanding the squared norm:

$$f(\mu_j) = \sum_{i \in C_j} \left( x_i^{\top} x_i - 2 x_i^{\top} \mu_j + \mu_j^{\top} \mu_j \right)$$

Taking the gradient with respect to $\mu_j$:

$$\nabla_{\mu_j} f(\mu_j) = \sum_{i \in C_j} \left( -2 x_i + 2 \mu_j \right) = 2 \left( \vert C_j \vert \, \mu_j - \sum_{i \in C_j} x_i \right)$$

Setting the gradient to zero (necessary condition for a minimum):

$$\vert C_j \vert \, \mu_j^{\ast} - \sum_{i \in C_j} x_i = 0$$

$$\boxed{\mu_j^{\ast} = \frac{1}{\vert C_j \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_j} x_i}$$

To confirm this is a **minimum**, examine the Hessian: $\nabla^2 f = 2 \vert C_j \vert \, I \succ 0$ (positive definite), so the critical point is indeed a global minimum for cluster $C_j$. *(2 marks for the gradient setup, 3 marks for the derivation, 1 mark for the Hessian verification, 1 mark for the final boxed answer.)*

**(b) Algorithm explanation (7 marks):**

The K-Means algorithm seeks to minimize the WCSS objective: *(1 mark)*

$$J = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \sum_{x_i \in C_j} \Vert x_i - \mu_j \Vert^2$$

**Algorithm steps:** *(3 marks)*
1. **Initialize** k centroids $\mu_1, \dots, \mu_k$ via K-Means++.
2. **Assignment step:** $C_j = \{x_i : j = \arg\min_r \Vert x_i - \mu_r \Vert^2\}$.
3. **Update step:** $\mu_j = \frac{1}{\vert C_j \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_j} x_i$.
4. **Check convergence:** $\vert J^{(t+1)} - J^{(t)} \vert \le \varepsilon$. If not converged, return to step 2.

**K-Means++ initialization role:** *(2 marks)*

Standard random initialization may place multiple centroids in the same true cluster, leading to a poor local minimum and empty clusters. K-Means++ seeds centroids one at a time, with probability proportional to $D(x_i)^2$ (squared distance to nearest existing seed), ensuring diverse coverage of the data manifold. This gives an expected O(log k) competitive solution.

**Biological example:** *(1 mark)*

In scRNA-seq analysis, K-Means is applied to the PCA-reduced expression matrix to group cells into putative cell types, which are then annotated using marker genes.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Alternative Choice]*

**(a)** Explain the K-Medoids (PAM) algorithm. How does it differ from K-Means in handling outliers and arbitrary distance metrics? Discuss its computational complexity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the Fuzzy C-Means algorithm? Derive the membership and cluster center update equations. Mention one application in computational biology. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) K-Medoids / PAM (7 marks):**

The **K-Medoids** algorithm (Kaufman & Rousseeuw, 1987) replaces the centroid (mean) of K-Means with a **medoid** — the actual data point in a cluster that minimizes the total dissimilarity to all other points in that cluster. *(1 mark)*

**PAM objective:** *(1 mark)*

$$J_{\text{PAM}} = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \sum_{x_i \in C_j} d(x_i, m_j), \quad m_j \in C_j$$

**Algorithm steps:** *(2 marks)*
1. **Build phase:** greedily select $k$ medoids that minimize the total cost.
2. **Swap phase:** for each (medoid, non-medoid) pair, evaluate the cost change of swapping and accept the swap if it reduces the cost. Iterate until convergence.

**Outlier handling:** K-Medoids is **robust to outliers** because the medoid is a *bounded* statistic — the medoid is always an actual data point, and replacing a noisy point with a typical point does not "drag" the medoid to extremes (unlike the mean). *(1 mark)*

**Arbitrary distance metrics:** Since the algorithm operates on a pairwise dissimilarity matrix, it can use **non-Euclidean, non-metric** distances such as edit (Levenshtein) distance for DNA/protein sequence clustering — a major advantage in computational biology. *(1 mark)*

**Complexity:** The swap phase evaluates $O(k(n-k))$ swap candidates, each requiring $O(n)$ cost recomputation, giving $O(k(n-k)^2)$ per PAM iteration — substantially slower than K-Means ($O(nkd)$ per iteration). This is why **CLARA** (sampling) and **CLARANS** (randomized graph search) are used for large datasets. *(1 mark)*

**(b) Fuzzy C-Means (7 marks):**

**Idea:** Each datum $x_i$ has a graded membership $u_{ij} \in [0,1]$ in each cluster, with the constraint $\sum_{j=1}^{k} u_{ij} = 1$ for all $i$. *(1 mark)*

**Objective:** *(1 mark)*

$$J_{FCM} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{k} u_{ij}^{m} \Vert x_i - v_j \Vert^2, \quad m > 1$$

where $m$ is the **fuzzifier** controlling cluster softness (typically $m=2$).

**Derivation of update equations:** *(3 marks)*

Minimize $J_{FCM}$ subject to $\sum_j u_{ij} = 1$ using Lagrange multipliers:

$$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{k} u_{ij}^{m} \Vert x_i - v_j \Vert^2 - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i \left( \sum_{j=1}^{k} u_{ij} - 1 \right)$$

Setting $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial u_{ij} = 0$ and $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial v_j = 0$:

$$m \, u_{ij}^{m-1} \Vert x_i - v_j \Vert^2 = \lambda_i \quad \Longrightarrow \quad u_{ij} = \left( \frac{\lambda_i}{m \Vert x_i - v_j \Vert^2} \right)^{\frac{1}{m-1}}$$

Applying the constraint $\sum_j u_{ij} = 1$ and solving for $\lambda_i$:

$$\boxed{u_{ij} = \left[ \sum_{r=1}^{k} \left( \frac{\Vert x_i - v_j \Vert}{\Vert x_i - v_r \Vert} \right)^{\frac{2}{m-1}} \right]^{-1}}$$

For the cluster centers (unconstrained minimization):

$$\boxed{v_j = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} u_{ij}^{m} x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} u_{ij}^{m}}}$$

**Application in computational biology:** *(2 marks)*

Fuzzy C-Means is used in **gene expression clustering** when cell populations are genuinely *continua* (e.g., differentiating stem cells, where the boundary between "pluripotent" and "mesoderm" cells is fuzzy). Soft memberships provide a more biologically faithful description than hard assignments. It is also used in **brain tissue segmentation from MRI** where voxel boundaries are inherently gradual.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Watch Out!)**
> 1. **Confusing centroid with medoid.** Writing "the centroid is the closest data point" will cost you full marks. Centroid = mean (synthetic), medoid = actual datum minimizing total dissimilarity.
> 2. **Forgetting the squared norm.** WCSS uses $\Vert x_i - \mu_j \Vert^2$ (squared Euclidean), not $\Vert x_i - \mu_j \Vert$. Examiners notice.
> 3. **Skipping the Hessian check** in the centroid derivation — the gradient-zero condition is necessary but not sufficient; you must verify positive-definiteness (or at least state it) to claim a minimum.
> 4. **Not specifying convergence criterion.** Always mention $\vert J^{(t+1)} - J^{(t)} \vert \le \varepsilon$ or a maximum-iteration stop rule.
> 5. **Confusing K-Means with hierarchical clustering.** Hierarchical methods produce a dendrogram (no pre-specified k); K-Means requires k upfront and produces a flat partition.
> 6. **Mixing up K-Means++ with random initialization.** Random = uniform over data; K-Means++ = proportional to $D(x_i)^2$.
> 7. **Ignoring data preprocessing.** K-Means assumes features are on comparable scales. Always normalize/standardize before clustering — failure to do so will silently degrade results in high-dimensional biological data.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Partition clustering = divide $n$ points into $k$ non-overlapping, exhaustive subsets.
- **Three axioms:** $\bigcup C_j = X$, $C_i \cap C_j = \emptyset$, $C_j \neq \emptyset$.
- **K-Means objective:** $J = \sum_j \sum_{x_i \in C_j} \Vert x_i - \mu_j \Vert^2$ (WCSS).
- **Centroid update:** $\mu_j = \frac{1}{\vert C_j \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_j} x_i$ (rigorously derived from gradient-zero).
- **Assignment step:** Argmin of squared Euclidean distance to centroids.
- **K-Means++:** Probability of choosing next centroid $\propto D(x_i)^2$; O(log k) competitive.
- **Total scatter identity:** $S_T = S_B + S_W$ — minimizing $S_W$ = maximizing $S_B$.
- **K-Medoids (PAM):** Uses actual data points as medoids; robust to outliers; supports any dissimilarity (e.g., edit distance for sequences).
- **K-Medoids complexity:** $O(k(n-k)^2)$ per iteration; for large n, use **CLARA** or **CLARANS**.
- **Fuzzy C-Means:** Soft partition with $u_{ij}$ membership grades; $u_{ij} = [\sum_r (\Vert x_i - v_j \Vert / \Vert x_i - v_r \Vert)^{2/(m-1)}]^{-1}$.
- **Convergence:** $J$ is monotonically non-increasing and bounded below $\Rightarrow$ guaranteed convergence to a **local** minimum (not necessarily global).
- **Choosing k:** Elbow method on $J$ vs $k$ plot; Silhouette coefficient $s(i) = (b(i) - a(i)) / \max(a(i), b(i))$.
- **Bio applications:** scRNA-seq cell-type identification, gene co-expression modules, protein family clustering, patient stratification from omics data, metagenomic binning via k-mer frequencies.
- **Preprocessing is mandatory:** Standardize features (zero mean, unit variance) before K-Means; biological features often have very different scales (e.g., gene counts vs. fold changes).
- **Limitations:** Assumes spherical, equal-variance clusters (K-Means); sensitive to initialization; cannot discover non-convex shapes; sensitive to outliers (K-Means specifically).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview of Model-Based Clustering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT633 - MACHINE LEARNING IN COMPUTATIONAL BIOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Overview of Model-Based Clustering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Model-Based Clustering** is a principled statistical framework for unsupervised learning in which data points are assumed to be generated from a *finite mixture of probability distributions*. Each component distribution in the mixture represents one cluster, parameterized by its own set of parameters (mean vector, covariance matrix, and mixing weight). The cluster identity of any data point is treated as a **latent (hidden) random variable**, and inference proceeds by jointly estimating the mixture parameters and the posterior distribution of these latent variables.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> Model-based clustering is a parametric approach to clustering in which the observed data $\mathbf{X} = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_N\}$ is modelled as a realisation of a probability density function of the form:
> $$p(x) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k \, f_k(x \mid \theta_k)$$
> where $\pi_k$ is the **mixing proportion** of component $k$, $f_k(\cdot)$ is the component density (typically a multivariate Gaussian), and $\theta_k$ is the parameter set of component $k$. The model is fitted by **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)**, usually via the **Expectation–Maximization (EM) algorithm**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Explanation

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "Birdwatchers in a Misty Forest"**
>
> Imagine you are standing in a dense forest on a foggy morning. You **cannot see** the birds directly, but you **hear** their calls from various directions. Each bird species has a *distinctive call pattern* (a probability distribution). Your job is to figure out:
> 1. How many species (clusters $K$) are out there?
> 2. What is the typical call pattern of each species (component parameters $\mu_k, \Sigma_k$)?
> 3. Which call you are currently hearing belongs to which species (posterior responsibility $\gamma(z_{nk})$)?
>
> You do not know the species labels in advance — you only have the audio signal (the observed data). Model-based clustering does exactly this: it **infers the underlying probabilistic generators** of the data without being told the labels. This is the essence of treating clustering as a *density estimation* problem rather than a geometric partitioning problem.

## 1.3 Why Model-Based Clustering Matters in Computational Biology

In computational biology, the objects we cluster — gene expression profiles, protein sequences, single-cell RNA-seq counts, phylogenetic distances, or population genotypes — are **noisy, high-dimensional, and inherently stochastic**. Geometric methods like K-Means force hard partitions and assume spherical, equally-sized clusters. Biological reality, however, often exhibits:

- **Overlapping clusters** (e.g., transitional cell states in differentiation)
- **Varying cluster shapes** (elongated, correlated, anisotropic distributions)
- **Unequal cluster sizes** (some cell types are abundant, others rare)
- **Probabilistic membership** (a cell may be 70% Type A, 30% Type B)

Model-based clustering addresses all of these by providing a *probabilistic, generative* framework.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The most widely used specialisation of model-based clustering in computational biology is the **Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM)**, in which each $f_k(\cdot)$ is a multivariate normal density. GMMs underpin tools like **mclust** (R package), widely cited in the original KTU 2024 reference for this module.

## 1.4 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Value / Range |
|---|---|---|
| $K$ | Number of mixture components (clusters) | $1 \le K \le K_{\max}$ |
| $N$ | Number of observed data points | $\mathbb{Z}^+$ |
| $d$ | Dimensionality of each data point | $\mathbb{Z}^+$ |
| $\pi_k$ | Mixing proportion of component $k$ | $0 \le \pi_k \le 1$, $\sum_k \pi_k = 1$ |
| $\mu_k$ | Mean vector of component $k$ | $\mathbb{R}^d$ |
| $\Sigma_k$ | Covariance matrix of component $k$ | $\mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$, positive-definite |
| $z_{nk}$ | Latent indicator: point $n$ belongs to component $k$ | $z_{nk} \in \{0, 1\}$ |
| $\gamma(z_{nk})$ | Posterior responsibility of $k$ for point $n$ | $0 \le \gamma \le 1$, $\sum_k \gamma = 1$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Finite Mixture Model — Component-by-Component Breakdown

A **finite mixture model** asserts that the data-generation process is a weighted sum of $K$ component densities. Let us break down each element of this construct:

### Step 1 — Component Density $f_k(x \mid \theta_k)$

For computational biology, the canonical choice is the **multivariate Gaussian (Normal) density**:

$$f_k(x \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{d/2} \, \vert \Sigma_k \vert^{1/2}} \, \exp\!\left(-\tfrac{1}{2}(x-\mu_k)^{T} \Sigma_k^{-1} (x-\mu_k)\right)$$

- The **Mahalanobis distance** term $(x-\mu_k)^T \Sigma_k^{-1} (x-\mu_k)$ measures how many "standard deviations" point $x$ is from the mean, accounting for the cluster's shape and orientation.
- $\vert \Sigma_k \vert$ is the **determinant**, which normalises the density so it integrates to 1.

### Step 2 — Mixing Proportions $\pi_k$

These are the *prior probabilities* of belonging to each component before observing the data. They satisfy two constraints:
1. **Non-negativity:** $\pi_k \ge 0 \quad \forall k$
2. **Normalisation:** $\sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k = 1$

Interpretation: $\pi_k$ is the *expected fraction* of the population belonging to cluster $k$.

### Step 3 — Latent Variables $z_n$ (The Hidden Cluster Labels)

For each data point $x_n$, we define a 1-of-$K$ binary vector $z_n = (z_{n1}, \dots, z_{nK})$ where exactly one entry is 1. Marginalising over $z_n$ yields the mixture density:

$$p(x_n) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} p(z_{nk}=1) \, p(x_n \mid z_{nk}=1) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k \, f_k(x_n \mid \theta_k)$$

### Step 4 — Likelihood Function

Assuming **i.i.d.** data points, the observed-data log-likelihood is:

$$\ell(\theta) = \log p(\mathbf{X} \mid \theta) = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \log \left( \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k \, f_k(x_n \mid \theta_k) \right)$$

The **log-sum inside the sum** is what makes direct MLE intractable — it has no closed-form solution, which is precisely why we need the **EM algorithm**.

### Step 5 — Complete-Data Likelihood (with Latents)

If we *knew* the $z_{nk}$ values, the complete-data log-likelihood would factorise nicely:

$$\ell_c(\theta) = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} z_{nk} \left[ \log \pi_k + \log f_k(x_n \mid \theta_k) \right]$$

This factorisation is the foundation of the EM algorithm's **M-step**, where we replace $z_{nk}$ with its expected value $\gamma(z_{nk})$.

## 2.2 The EM Algorithm for GMMs — Operational Logic

The **Expectation–Maximization (EM) algorithm** is the workhorse for fitting mixture models. It is an *iterative, two-step* procedure guaranteed to monotonically increase (or maintain) the observed-data log-likelihood.

### E-Step (Expectation)

Compute the **posterior responsibility** of component $k$ for each data point $n$, given the *current* parameter estimates $\theta^{\text{old}}$:

$$\gamma(z_{nk}) = \frac{\pi_k^{\text{old}} \, f_k(x_n \mid \mu_k^{\text{old}}, \Sigma_k^{\text{old}})}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} \pi_j^{\text{old}} \, f_j(x_n \mid \mu_j^{\text{old}}, \Sigma_j^{\text{old}})}$$

Then compute the **expected complete-data log-likelihood** (the "Q-function"):

$$Q(\theta, \theta^{\text{old}}) = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \gamma(z_{nk}) \left[ \log \pi_k + \log f_k(x_n \mid \theta_k) \right]$$

### M-Step (Maximisation)

Maximise $Q(\theta, \theta^{\text{old}})$ with respect to $\theta = \{\pi_k, \mu_k, \Sigma_k\}_{k=1}^{K}$ to obtain updated parameters:

$$\mu_k^{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{N_k} \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk}) \, x_n \quad \text{where} \quad N_k = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk})$$

$$\Sigma_k^{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{N_k} \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk}) \, (x_n - \mu_k^{\text{new}})(x_n - \mu_k^{\text{new}})^T$$

$$\pi_k^{\text{new}} = \frac{N_k}{N}$$

These are **weighted MLEs** where each point is weighted by its posterior responsibility — points that the model currently believes strongly belong to cluster $k$ pull the estimate of $\mu_k$ and $\Sigma_k$ more strongly.

### Convergence Check

Iterate E-step $\leftrightarrow$ M-step until any of the following:

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Convergence Criteria:**
> 1. **Log-likelihood change:** $\vert \ell^{(t+1)} - \ell^{(t)} \vert < \epsilon$ (e.g., $\epsilon = 10^{-6}$)
> 2. **Parameter change:** $\Vert \theta^{(t+1)} - \theta^{(t)} \Vert < \epsilon$
> 3. **Maximum iterations reached:** $t = T_{\max}$ (e.g., $T_{\max} = 100$)

## 2.3 Choosing the Number of Components $K$ — Model Selection

The number of clusters $K$ is itself a hyperparameter. Three principled criteria are commonly used:

| Criterion | Formula | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **Likelihood** | $\ell(\hat{\theta}_K)$ | Higher is better; but increases monotonically with $K$ |
| **AIC (Akaike)** | $-2 \ell(\hat{\theta}_K) + 2 p_K$ | Penalises model complexity by $2p$ |
| **BIC (Bayesian)** | $-2 \ell(\hat{\theta}_K) + p_K \log N$ | Stronger penalty; consistent estimator of $K$ |

where $p_K$ is the **number of free parameters** in a $K$-component GMM in $d$ dimensions:

$$p_K = K \cdot \left(1 + d + \frac{d(d+1)}{2}\right) + (K-1)$$

The terms inside the parentheses represent: 1 for the mean scale (not really, but the convention follows), $d$ for the mean vector, $\frac{d(d+1)}{2}$ for the symmetric covariance matrix, and $(K-1)$ for the free mixing proportions (the last is determined by the constraint).

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** In bioinformatics pipelines (e.g., clustering flow-cytometry data or scRNA-seq), BIC is the **de facto standard** because it tends to select parsimonious models — a critical property when cluster identity is later used to define cell types with biological names.

## 2.4 Covariance Matrix Parameterisations (mclust Convention)

Different biological questions require different assumptions about cluster shape. The **mclust** package in R (Fraley & Raftery) parameterises covariance matrices using an **EII / VII / EEI / VEI / EVI / VVI / EEE / VEE / EVV / VVV** nomenclature:

| Code | Volume | Shape | Orientation | Free Parameters |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **EII** | Equal | Spherical | — | 1 |
| **VII** | Variable | Spherical | — | $K$ |
| **EEE** | Equal | Equal | Equal | $d(d+1)/2 + d$ |
| **VVV** | Variable | Variable | Variable | $K \cdot d(d+1)/2$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Favourite:** The **VVV** model is the most general (no constraints on $\Sigma_k$) and is what most students will fit by default. The **EII** model reduces to the spherical K-Means analogue.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Key Insight |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mixture density | $p(x) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k f_k(x \mid \theta_k)$ | Linear combination of component densities |
| 2 | Multivariate Gaussian | $f_k(x) = (2\pi)^{-d/2}\vert\Sigma_k\vert^{-1/2}\exp(-\frac{1}{2}\Delta^2)$ | $\Delta^2 = (x-\mu_k)^T\Sigma_k^{-1}(x-\mu_k)$ is the Mahalanobis distance |
| 3 | Prior on cluster | $p(z_{nk}=1) = \pi_k$ | Mixing weight = prior probability |
| 4 | Posterior responsibility (E-step) | $\gamma(z_{nk}) = \frac{\pi_k f_k(x_n)}{\sum_j \pi_j f_j(x_n)}$ | Normalised likelihood-weighted prior |
| 5 | Effective component size | $N_k = \sum_n \gamma(z_{nk})$ | Soft count of points in component $k$ |
| 6 | Updated mean (M-step) | $\mu_k = \frac{1}{N_k}\sum_n \gamma(z_{nk}) x_n$ | Responsibility-weighted sample mean |
| 7 | Updated covariance (M-step) | $\Sigma_k = \frac{1}{N_k}\sum_n \gamma(z_{nk})(x_n-\mu_k)(x_n-\mu_k)^T$ | Responsibility-weighted sample covariance |
| 8 | Updated mixing weight | $\pi_k = N_k / N$ | Proportional to soft count |
| 9 | BIC for model selection | $\mathrm{BIC} = -2\ell + p_K \log N$ | Lower is better; $p_K$ is # free parameters |
| 10 | # free parameters in GMM | $p_K = K(d + d(d+1)/2 + 1) - 1$ | Per-component + mixing constraints |
| 11 | Log-likelihood (observed) | $\ell = \sum_n \log \sum_k \pi_k f_k(x_n)$ | Log-sum inside sum (no closed form) |
| 12 | Complete-data log-likelihood | $\ell_c = \sum_n \sum_k z_{nk}[\log\pi_k + \log f_k(x_n)]$ | Factors nicely over components |
