<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Paper Style]**

### Q1. Define patient subtype identification using clustering. Mention any two clustering algorithms used in this context.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Patient subtype identification using clustering is the process of applying unsupervised learning algorithms to high-dimensional biological data (e.g., gene expression, methylation, proteomic profiles) to discover intrinsic groupings of patients that share similar molecular or clinical phenotypes, without using any pre-assigned class labels. The output is a partition of the patient cohort into $K$ clusters, each representing a candidate subtype. Two commonly used algorithms are: **(1) K-Means clustering**, which partitions patients into $K$ non-overlapping groups by minimizing the within-cluster sum of squared Euclidean distances to centroids, and **(2) Agglomerative Hierarchical Clustering**, which builds a dendrogram by successively merging the two most similar patients/clusters according to a linkage criterion (single, complete, average, or Ward).

### Q2. What is the silhouette coefficient? State its range and the interpretation of its extreme values.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The silhouette coefficient is an internal cluster validation metric that quantifies how well each patient fits within its assigned cluster relative to other clusters. For a patient $i$, it is computed as

$$s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$$

where $a(i)$ is the mean distance to other patients in the same cluster and $b(i)$ is the smallest mean distance to any other cluster. The range is $[-1, +1]$. A value close to $+1$ indicates that the patient is well-clustered (much closer to its own cluster than to neighboring ones); a value near $0$ indicates that the patient lies on the boundary between two clusters; and a value near $-1$ suggests that the patient may have been assigned to the wrong cluster.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 + KTU ESE 2024 Expected Pattern]**

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — Clustering Algorithms & Mathematical Foundations

**Q.A (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the step-by-step working of the K-Means clustering algorithm for identifying patient subtypes. State its objective function and prove the centroid update rule. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

K-Means is an iterative, centroid-based clustering algorithm. The pipeline is:

1. **Input:** Patient-feature matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p}$, desired number of subtypes $K$.
2. **Initialization:** Choose $K$ initial centroids $\mu_1, \ldots, \mu_K$ (randomly, or via k-means++).
3. **Assignment Step:** For each patient $x_i$, assign it to the cluster whose centroid is nearest in Euclidean distance:

$$c_i \leftarrow \arg\min_{k} \|x_i - \mu_k\|^2$$

4. **Update Step:** Recompute each centroid as the mean of all patients currently assigned to its cluster:

$$\mu_k \leftarrow \frac{1}{|C_k|}\sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$$

5. **Convergence Check:** Repeat Steps 3 and 4 until centroids stabilize or a maximum number of iterations is reached.

**Objective Function (the quantity being minimized):**

$$\mathcal{J} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \|x_i - \mu_k\|^2$$

**Proof of the Centroid Update Rule:**

For a fixed cluster assignment $C_k$, take the gradient of $\mathcal{J}$ with respect to $\mu_k$ and set it to zero:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{J}}{\partial \mu_k} = -2 \sum_{x_i \in C_k} (x_i - \mu_k) = 0$$

Solving:

$$\sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i = |C_k| \mu_k \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \mu_k = \frac{1}{|C_k|}\sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$$

This proves the centroid update rule, and the second derivative is $2|C_k| \cdot I_{p \times p} \succ 0$, confirming that the update yields a minimum of $\mathcal{J}$ for the fixed assignment. The two alternating steps are guaranteed to monotonically decrease $\mathcal{J}$, and since $\mathcal{J}$ is bounded below by zero, the algorithm converges to a local minimum.

---

**Q.A (b)** Compare K-Means, Hierarchical Clustering, and DBSCAN for patient subtyping on the basis of (i) requirement to pre-specify $K$, (ii) cluster shape assumption, (iii) handling of outliers, and (iv) computational complexity. Which would you choose for discovering novel, irregularly shaped subtypes in single-cell RNA-seq data, and why? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

| Criterion | K-Means | Hierarchical | DBSCAN |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Pre-specify $K$? | **Yes** | **No** (produces dendrogram) | **No** (chosen via $\varepsilon$, MinPts) |
| (ii) Cluster shape | Assumes **convex, spherical** | Flexible (depends on linkage) | Detects **arbitrary, irregular** shapes |
| (iii) Outlier handling | **Sensitive** (forces assignment) | **Sensitive** (chain effect in single linkage) | **Robust** (marks noise as $-1$) |
| (iv) Complexity | $O(n \cdot K \cdot I \cdot p)$ | $O(n^3)$ naive / $O(n^2 \log n)$ optimized | $O(n \log n)$ with indexing |

**Choice for single-cell RNA-seq subtype discovery:** **DBSCAN (or its hierarchical extension HDBSCAN).**

**Justification:**

1. Single-cell RNA-seq data is **highly sparse and noisy**, with a long tail of low-quality cells and doublets that act as outliers. DBSCAN's ability to explicitly label such points as noise (cluster label $-1$) is invaluable.
2. Novel cell types may occupy **non-spherical, irregular niches** in gene-expression space (e.g., elongated manifolds). DBSCAN's density-based definition naturally conforms to such shapes, while K-Means would erroneously split them.
3. The number of cell types is **unknown a priori**, and the dendrogram from hierarchical clustering becomes computationally infeasible for the typical $n \in [10^4, 10^6]$ cells in modern scRNA-seq experiments.
4. For very large datasets, **HDBSCAN** (hierarchical DBSCAN) is preferred as it removes the global $\varepsilon$ sensitivity while retaining the irregular-shape advantage.

Therefore, DBSCAN/HDBSCAN is the principled choice for novel, irregularly shaped subtype discovery in scRNA-seq patient data.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Validation, Stability, and Clinical Translation

**Q.B (a)** Explain the concept of consensus clustering in patient subtype identification. How is the consensus matrix constructed, and what property of the matrix indicates a robust, biologically meaningful set of subtypes? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Concept:**
Consensus clustering is a stability-based resampling framework designed to overcome the inherent instability of single-run clustering algorithms (e.g., the random-initialization sensitivity of K-Means). It produces a subtype assignment that is robust to small perturbations in the input patient data, and therefore more likely to reflect genuine biological structure rather than algorithmic artifact.

**Construction of the Consensus Matrix:**

1. The patient-feature matrix $X$ is resampled $R$ times (e.g., by bootstrap subsampling of patients, or by subsampling of features) yielding datasets $X^{(1)}, X^{(2)}, \ldots, X^{(R)}$.
2. On each resampled dataset, a base clustering algorithm is run (e.g., K-Means with $K$ clusters), producing a cluster assignment $c^{(r)} = (c_1^{(r)}, c_2^{(r)}, \ldots, c_n^{(r)})$.
3. The consensus matrix $M \in [0,1]^{n \times n}$ is defined entry-wise as:

$$M_{ij} = \frac{1}{R}\sum_{r=1}^{R} \mathbb{1}\{c_i^{(r)} = c_j^{(r)}\}$$

i.e., $M_{ij}$ is the empirical probability (fraction of runs) that patients $i$ and $j$ were assigned to the same cluster.

**Property Indicating Robust Subtypes:**

A robust, biologically meaningful set of subtypes manifests as a **cleanly block-diagonal consensus matrix** — that is, $M$ contains $K$ well-separated square blocks of entries near $1$ (patients *within* a subtype co-cluster consistently), and off-block entries near $0$ (patients across subtypes co-cluster rarely). The boundaries between blocks are sharp, and the empirical cumulative distribution function (CDF) of $M$ shows a characteristic "bimodal" shape with a flat middle plateau whose length correlates with the optimal number of subtypes $K$. A consensus matrix that is uniformly gray (no block structure) indicates that the data has no stable clustering tendency at that $K$.

---

**Q.B (b)** Describe the silhouette method for validating patient subtypes. Given a 4-patient dataset with pairwise Euclidean distances as follows: $d(x_1,x_2)=2, d(x_1,x_3)=8, d(x_1,x_4)=8, d(x_2,x_3)=9, d(x_2,x_4)=9, d(x_3,x_4)=1$. Suppose clustering assigns $C_1=\{x_1,x_2\}$ and $C_2=\{x_3,x_4\}$. Compute the silhouette coefficient for $x_1$ and interpret. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**The Silhouette Method:**

For each patient $i$ in cluster $C_a$, compute:
- $a(i) = $ mean distance from $i$ to all other points in $C_a$.
- $b(i) = $ minimum, over all other clusters $C_b$, of the mean distance from $i$ to all points in $C_b$.

Then:

$$s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$$

The mean silhouette $\bar{S}$ over all $n$ patients is used to choose the optimal $K$ and to validate cluster quality.

**Computation for $x_1$:**

Step 1 — Compute $a(x_1)$:
$x_1$ is in $C_1 = \{x_1, x_2\}$. Mean distance to the other point in its cluster:

$$a(x_1) = d(x_1, x_2) = 2$$

Step 2 — Compute $b(x_1)$:
$x_1$ is not in $C_2 = \{x_3, x_4\}$. Mean distance to points in $C_2$:

$$b(x_1) = \frac{d(x_1,x_3) + d(x_1,x_4)}{2} = \frac{8+8}{2} = 8$$

Step 3 — Compute $s(x_1)$:

$$s(x_1) = \frac{b(x_1) - a(x_1)}{\max\{a(x_1), b(x_1)\}} = \frac{8 - 2}{\max\{2, 8\}} = \frac{6}{8} = 0.75$$

**Interpretation:**

A silhouette value of $s(x_1) = 0.75$ is **close to $+1$**, indicating that patient $x_1$ is **well-clustered** in $C_1$. The intra-cluster distance (2) is substantially smaller than the distance to the nearest other cluster (8), so the partition $C_1, C_2$ is meaningful for this patient. In practice, the mean silhouette across all patients would be computed and compared across multiple $K$ to select the optimal number of subtypes.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in the KTU Board Exam**
> 1. **Confusing the centroid with the median** — centroids are arithmetic means, not medians. Mean is required to derive the WCSS minimization.
> 2. **Forgetting to standardize features** — if features are on different scales, Euclidean distance is dominated by the largest-scale feature. The board will deduct 1 mark if standardization is omitted from the pre-processing step.
> 3. **Writing K-Means++ initialization without explaining it** — the standard k-means is sensitive to initialization. Always mention **k-means++** as the default in modern implementations.
> 4. **Reporting the Silhouette range as $[0, 1]$** — incorrect. The true range is $[-1, +1]$. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this.
> 5. **Using K-Means on non-spherical data** — this is a classic conceptual error. Justify the choice of algorithm based on data geometry.
> 6. **Confusing WCSS with TSS** — WCSS measures within-cluster compactness; TSS (Total Sum of Squares) is constant and equals WCSS + BCSS. Examiners frequently test this decomposition.
> 7. **Neglecting biological validation** — a clustering solution is not complete without clinical/survival/pathway validation. The board expects this step in any 14-mark answer.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Review)**

- ☐ Patient subtype identification is an **unsupervised learning** task — no labels are used during clustering.
- ☐ The patient-feature matrix $X$ has patients as rows and genes/features as columns; always **standardize** before clustering.
- ☐ The **curse of dimensionality** demands feature selection or dimensionality reduction (PCA, DE-genes) before clustering.
- ☐ The **K-Means objective** is $\mathcal{J} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \|x_i - \mu_k\|^2$ and is minimized by alternating assignment + centroid update.
- ☐ The centroid is the **mean** of cluster members; the centroid update rule is **derived** by setting $\partial \mathcal{J}/\partial \mu_k = 0$.
- ☐ The **Silhouette coefficient** $s(i) = \dfrac{b(i)-a(i)}{\max\{a(i),b(i)\}}$ lies in $[-1, +1]$; higher is better.
- ☐ **Davies-Bouldin** is minimized; **Calinski-Harabasz** is maximized; both compare within vs between-cluster dispersion.
- ☐ The **elbow method** on WCSS-vs-$K$ is heuristic; the **silhouette** or **gap statistic** are more rigorous for choosing $K$.
- ☐ **Consensus clustering** measures stability across resampled runs; a **block-diagonal** consensus matrix indicates robust subtypes.
- ☐ **Hierarchical clustering** does not require pre-specifying $K$ but is $O(n^2 \log n)$; **DBSCAN** handles noise and arbitrary shapes.
- ☐ Always perform **biological validation** — Kaplan-Meier survival curves, GSEA pathway enrichment, drug response assays.
- ☐ KTU frequently asks: *justify algorithm choice* — match algorithm to data shape, scale, and biological question.
- ☐ KTU frequently asks: *compute silhouette numerically* — practice the 3-step calculation ($a(i) \to b(i) \to s(i)$).
- ☐ KTU frequently asks: *define and interpret WCSS, BCSS, TSS decomposition* — these appear in nearly every recent ESE paper.
- ☐ KTU frequently asks: *what is consensus clustering and how is $M_{ij}$ computed?* — memorize the indicator-sum formula.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application of Clustering Algorithms on Gene Expression Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT633 - MACHINE LEARNING IN COMPUTATIONAL BIOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Application of Clustering Algorithms on Gene Expression Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gene Expression Data** is a high-dimensional numerical matrix obtained from high-throughput transcriptomic technologies (DNA microarrays and RNA-Seq), in which rows represent **genes** (probes/ transcripts), columns represent **experimental conditions** (tissues, time points, patients, or treatments), and each cell $(i, j)$ stores the **expression level** (e.g., normalized log-intensity, TPM, or FPKM) of gene $i$ under condition $j$.

Formally, let $\mathbf{E} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$ be the expression matrix with $n$ genes and $m$ conditions. Each row vector $\mathbf{g}_i \in \mathbb{R}^m$ is a **gene expression profile**, and each column vector $\mathbf{c}_j \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is a **sample expression signature**.

**Clustering on gene expression data** is the unsupervised partitioning of either genes (rows) or samples (columns) into homogeneous groups such that members of the same group share coherent expression behavior, while members of different groups exhibit divergent behavior. Two complementary objectives exist:

- **Gene clustering (co-expression analysis):** group genes with similar expression profiles $\mathbf{g}_i$ across conditions.
- **Sample clustering (subtype discovery):** group conditions $\mathbf{c}_j$ (e.g., patients) with similar transcriptional signatures.

> [!NOTE]
> **Course Outcome Mapping (KTU 2024):** This topic directly satisfies **CO2** – *Apply unsupervised machine learning algorithms to high-dimensional biological data and interpret biologically meaningful patterns.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **school auditorium with 20,000 students** (genes), each holding a coloured flag whose height changes across 100 different weather conditions (samples). You want to find:
- *Which students wave their flags in unison?* (co-expressed genes → functional complexes / pathways).
- *Which groups of weather days look the same from the flag patterns?* (sample clusters → disease subtypes or treatment states).

**Clustering algorithms** are the "librarian algorithms" that, without being told the answer, sort the noisy high-dimensional data into biologically interpretable bookshelves.

> [!TIP]
> **Why clustering on gene expression?** *The "guilt-by-association" principle*: genes with similar expression patterns across many conditions are likely to participate in the same biological process, pathway, or regulatory module. Clustering is the computational realization of this biological hypothesis.

## 1.3 Why Standard Clustering Fails naively — The "Curse of Dimensionality" Warning

Gene expression matrices are characterised by:
- $n$ (genes) $\gg m$ (samples) → **small $n$, large $p$ problem**.
- Substantial **noise** (technical and biological variance).
- **Multi-functionality** — one gene may belong to several pathways.

This necessitates specialised similarity measures (Pearson/Spearman correlation) and algorithms tolerant of noise (SOM, fuzzy methods, biclustering).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pre-Clustering Pipeline (Mandatory Steps)

1. **Background correction & normalization** (RMA, quantile, TPM-log).
2. **Log-transformation** ($\log_2(\text{FPKM}+1)$) to stabilize variance.
3. **Filtering low-variance genes** (top $k$ most variable by MAD or IQR).
4. **Optional standardization** (z-score per gene): $z_{ij} = \dfrac{e_{ij} - \bar{\mathbf{g}}_i}{s_i}$.
5. **Similarity / Distance computation.**
6. **Algorithm execution.**
7. **Validation** (internal + biological).

## 2.2 Similarity & Distance Metrics

For two expression profiles $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^m$:

| Metric | Formula | When used in gene expression |
|---|---|---|
| **Euclidean** | $d_E(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y}) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{m}(x_j-y_j)^2}$ | Magnitude-sensitive; sample clustering |
| **Manhattan (L1)** | $d_1(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y}) = \sum_{j=1}^{m} \vert x_j - y_j \vert$ | Robust to outliers |
| **Pearson distance** | $d_P = 1 - r_P$, $r_P = \dfrac{\sum (x_j-\bar{x})(y_j-\bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum (x_j-\bar{x})^2 \sum (y_j-\bar{y})^2}}$ | **Most common for gene co-expression** (shape-based) |
| **Spearman distance** | $d_S = 1 - r_S$ (ranks) | Robust to non-linearity, small samples |
| **Cosine distance** | $d_C = 1 - \dfrac{\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{y}}{\vert\vert\mathbf{x}\vert\vert \,\vert\vert\mathbf{y}\vert\vert}$ | Direction similarity, magnitude-invariant |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pearson correlation is preferred for gene clustering** because it captures *shape similarity* of expression trajectories, ignoring scale. Genes co-regulated tend to have correlated patterns rather than identical absolute levels.

## 2.3 Major Clustering Algorithms Applied to Expression Data

### 2.3.1 K-Means Clustering
**Idea:** Partition $n$ items into $k$ clusters minimising within-cluster sum of squares (WCSS).

Objective:

$$J(\mathcal{C}) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in C_k} \vert\vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \vert\vert^2$$

**Iterative steps:**

- **Assignment:** $C_k^{(t)} = \left\{ \mathbf{x}_i : \vert\vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{(t)} \vert\vert^2 \le \vert\vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j^{(t)} \vert\vert^2 \, \forall j \right\}$
- **Update:** $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{(t+1)} = \dfrac{1}{\vert C_k^{(t)} \vert} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in C_k^{(t)}} \mathbf{x}_i$

**Pros:** fast $O(n K I m)$, scales to large expression datasets. **Cons:** requires $k$ upfront, spherical clusters, sensitive to initialisation (use k-means++), Euclidean only.

### 2.3.2 Hierarchical Clustering (HC)
**Idea:** Build a dendrogram either agglomeratively (bottom-up) or divisively (top-down).

**Linkage criteria** between clusters $A$ and $B$:

| Linkage | Definition |
|---|---|
| Single | $d(A,B) = \min_{\mathbf{x}\in A, \mathbf{y}\in B} d(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y})$ |
| Complete | $d(A,B) = \max_{\mathbf{x}\in A, \mathbf{y}\in B} d(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y})$ |
| Average (UPGMA) | $d(A,B) = \frac{1}{\vert A \vert \vert B \vert}\sum d(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y})$ |
| **Ward's** | Minimises increase in within-cluster SSE: $\Delta(A,B) = \dfrac{\vert A \vert \vert B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}\, \vert\vert \boldsymbol{\mu}_A - \boldsymbol{\mu}_B \vert\vert^2$ |

**Most used in gene expression:** Average linkage + Pearson distance → classic "Eisen plot" of co-expression.

### 2.3.3 Self-Organizing Maps (SOM)
A neural grid where each neuron $k$ has a prototype vector $\mathbf{w}_k \in \mathbb{R}^m$. On presenting gene $\mathbf{x}(t)$:

- **Winning neuron (BMU):** $c = \arg\min_k \vert\vert \mathbf{x}(t) - \mathbf{w}_k \vert\vert$
- **Update rule:** $\mathbf{w}_k(t+1) = \mathbf{w}_k(t) + \alpha(t)\, h_{ck}(t)\, [\mathbf{x}(t) - \mathbf{w}_k(t)]$

Where $h_{ck}(t) = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\vert\vert \mathbf{r}_c - \mathbf{r}_k \vert\vert^2}{2\sigma^2(t)}\right)$ is the neighbourhood function.

**Why SOM in expression data?** Projects $m$-dimensional expression space onto a 2-D grid preserving topology; widely used in cancer subtyping (Tamayo et al., 1999).

### 2.3.4 Fuzzy C-Means (FCM)
A gene may belong to multiple co-expression modules; FCM assigns *membership* $u_{ik} \in [0,1]$ with $\sum_k u_{ik} = 1$.

$$J_{FCM} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{k=1}^{c} u_{ik}^{\,q} \vert\vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{v}_k \vert\vert^2, \quad q>1 \text{ (fuzzifier)}$$

Update:

$$u_{ik} = \left[ \sum_{j=1}^{c} \left( \dfrac{\vert\vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{v}_k \vert\vert}{\vert\vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{v}_j \vert\vert} \right)^{\tfrac{2}{q-1}} \right]^{-1}, \quad \mathbf{v}_k = \dfrac{\sum_i u_{ik}^{\,q}\mathbf{x}_i}{\sum_i u_{ik}^{\,q}}$$

### 2.3.5 DBSCAN (Density-Based)
Defines clusters as dense regions separated by sparse regions. Two parameters: $\varepsilon$ (neighbourhood radius) and $\text{MinPts}$.

$$N_\varepsilon(\mathbf{x}) = \{\mathbf{y} \in \mathcal{D} : d(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y}) \le \varepsilon\}$$

- **Core point:** $\vert N_\varepsilon(\mathbf{x}) \vert \ge \text{MinPts}$.
- **Border point:** in core's neighbourhood but not core.
- **Noise point:** neither.

Useful for detecting outlier genes / rare cell populations in scRNA-seq.

### 2.3.6 Biclustering (Cheng & Church)
Standard clustering clusters either genes OR conditions. **Biclustering** clusters both simultaneously, finding submatrices where a subset of genes co-vary across a subset of conditions.

**Cheng & Church score** for bicluster $(I, J)$:

$$H(I,J) = \dfrac{1}{\vert I \vert \vert J \vert} \sum_{i\in I,\, j\in J} \left( e_{ij} - e_{iJ} - e_{Ij} + e_{IJ} \right)^2$$

Algorithms: Cheng & Church, Plaid model, FABIA, ISA, Spectral biclustering.

## 2.4 Cluster Validation Metrics (KTU Formula Sheet)

### Internal Validity (no ground truth)

**1. Silhouette Coefficient** for point $i$ in cluster $C_I$:

$$a(i) = \frac{1}{\vert C_I \vert - 1}\sum_{j \in C_I,\, j \neq i} d(i,j)$$

$$b(i) = \min_{K \neq I} \frac{1}{\vert C_K \vert}\sum_{j \in C_K} d(i,j)$$

$$s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i),\, b(i)\}}, \quad s \in [-1, 1]$$

Global: $S = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} s(i)$.

**2. Davies-Bouldin Index** (lower is better):

$$DB = \frac{1}{K}\sum_{i=1}^{K} \max_{j\neq i} \left[ \frac{S_i + S_j}{d(\boldsymbol{\mu}_i, \boldsymbol{\mu}_j)} \right]$$

where $S_i$ is average intra-cluster scatter.

**3. Calinski-Harabasz Index** (higher is better):

$$CH = \frac{ \text{SS}_B / (K-1) }{ \text{SS}_W / (n-K) } = \frac{\text{Tr}(B)\, (n-K)}{\text{Tr}(W)\, (K-1)}$$

with between-cluster and within-cluster scatter matrices $B$ and $W$.

**4. Gap Statistic (for choosing $K$):**

$$Gap(K) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\log \text{SS}_W(K^*)\right] - \log \text{SS}_W(K)$$

Choose smallest $K$ such that $Gap(K) \ge Gap(K+1) - s_{K+1}$.

### External Validity (with labels, e.g., known subtypes)

| Metric | Formula | Range |
|---|---|---|
| Rand Index | $RI = \dfrac{TP+TN}{\binom{n}{2}}$ | $[0,1]$, higher better |
| Adjusted RI | $ARI = \dfrac{RI - \mathbb{E}[RI]}{\max(RI) - \mathbb{E}[RI]}$ | $[-1,1]$ |
| Normalized Mutual Information | $NMI = \dfrac{2\,I(U;V)}{H(U)+H(V)}$ | $[0,1]$ |
| Fowlkes-Mallows | $FM = \dfrac{TP}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)}}$ | $[0,1]$ |

### Biological Validation
- **GO Enrichment** (hypergeometric test, Fisher's exact).
- **KEGG pathway over-representation.**
- **PPI network overlap** (STRING database).
- **Survival analysis** (Kaplan-Meier) for sample clusters in cancer.

## 2.5 Engineering / Real-World Utility

| Domain | Use of clustering on expression |
|---|---|
| **Oncology** | Identify novel cancer subtypes (e.g., PAM50 in breast cancer, Leukaemia ALL/AML) |
| **Pharmacology** | Predict drug response from baseline expression |
| **Developmental Biology** | Cluster time-series expression to find stage-specific modules |
| **Microbiome** | Cluster OTUs with co-abundance behaviour |
| **Single-cell RNA-seq** | Cell-type identification (Seurat uses graph + Louvain) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Parallel:** In bioinformatics pipelines, the same clustering logic appears in image segmentation, anomaly detection in IoT sensor arrays, and customer segmentation — clustering on expression is essentially high-dimensional unsupervised pattern recognition with biological semantics.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the K-Means Update Rule (Exhaustive)

**Goal:** Prove that fixing $C_k$, the centroid $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k$ that minimises $J = \sum_k \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in C_k} \vert\vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \vert\vert^2$ is the arithmetic mean of $C_k$.

Expand the squared norm:

$$\vert\vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \vert\vert^2 = \sum_{j=1}^{m} (x_{ij} - \mu_{kj})^2$$

Sum over $i \in C_k$:

$$J_k(\boldsymbol{\mu}_k) = \sum_{i \in C_k}\sum_{j=1}^{m}(x_{ij}-\mu_{kj})^2$$

Set partial derivative w.r.t. $\mu_{kj}$ to zero (first-order optimality condition):

$$\frac{\partial J_k}{\partial \mu_{kj}} = -2 \sum_{i \in C_k} (x_{ij} - \mu_{kj}) = 0$$

Solving:

$$\sum_{i \in C_k} x_{ij} - \vert C_k \vert \cdot \mu_{kj} = 0$$

$$\boxed{\mu_{kj} = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert}\sum_{i \in C_k} x_{ij}}$$

In vector form: $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k = \dfrac{1}{\vert C_k \vert}\sum_{i \in C_k} \mathbf{x}_i$. **QED.**

> **Interpretation:** The best representative of a cluster (in squared-error sense) is the centroid — the mean. Hence K-means always moves centroids to means of assigned points.

## 3.2 Derivation of Ward's Linkage

**Goal:** Show Ward's criterion minimises the increase in total within-cluster SSE when merging $A$ and $B$.

Let $SSE(A) = \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in A} \vert\vert \mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_A \vert\vert^2$. Total $T = SSE(A \cup B)$ before merge. After merging into $A \cup B$ with new mean $\boldsymbol{\mu}_{AB}$:

$$\Delta(A,B) = SSE(A \cup B) - SSE(A) - SSE(B)$$

Use the identity $\sum \vert\vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c} \vert\vert^2 = \sum \vert\vert \mathbf{x} - \bar{\mathbf{x}} \vert\vert^2 + \vert A \vert \vert\vert \bar{\mathbf{x}} - \mathbf{c} \vert\vert^2$ (parallel-axis theorem), with $\mathbf{c}$ being the original grand mean:

$$SSE(A \cup B) - SSE(A) - SSE(B) = \vert A \vert \vert\vert \boldsymbol{\mu}_A - \boldsymbol{\mu}_{AB} \vert\vert^2 + \vert B \vert \vert\vert \boldsymbol{\mu}_B - \boldsymbol{\mu}_{AB} \vert\vert^2$$

Substituting $\boldsymbol{\mu}_{AB} = \dfrac{\vert A \vert \boldsymbol{\mu}_A + \vert B \vert \boldsymbol{\mu}_B}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$:

$$\Delta(A,B) = \frac{\vert A \vert \vert B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert} \vert\vert \boldsymbol{\mu}_A - \boldsymbol{\mu}_B \vert\vert^2$$

This is **Ward's linkage**. It equals the squared Euclidean distance between centroids weighted by cluster sizes — favouring merges of small, similar clusters.

## 3.3 Silhouette Coefficient — Numerical Example

**Setup:** 5 expression profiles $\mathbf{x}_1, \dots, \mathbf{x}_5$ in $\mathbb{R}^2$ (toy), with clusters $C_1 = \{\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2\}$, $C_2 = \{\mathbf{x}_3, \mathbf{x}_4, \mathbf{x}_5\}$.

| Pair | $d$ |
|---|---|
| $d(\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2)$ | 1.0 |
| $d(\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_3)$ | 3.0 |
| $d(\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_4)$ | 4.0 |
| $d(\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_5)$ | 5.0 |
| $d(\mathbf{x}_3, \mathbf{x}_4)$ | 1.5 |
| $d(\mathbf{x}_3, \mathbf{x}_5)$ | 2.0 |
| $d(\mathbf{x}_4, \mathbf{x}_5)$ | 1.0 |

For $\mathbf{x}_1$: $a(1) = 1.0$, $b(1) = \frac{3.0+4.0+5.0}{3} = 4.0$.

$$s(1) = \frac{4.0 - 1.0}{\max(4.0, 1.0)} = \frac{3}{4} = 0.75$$

For $\mathbf{x}_3$: $a(3) = \frac{1.5+2.0}{2} = 1.75$, $b(3) = \frac{3.0+4.0+5.0}{2} = 6.0$ (next best single cluster — only $C_1$).

$$s(3) = \frac{6.0 - 1.75}{6.0} \approx 0.708$$

Both well-clustered ($s > 0$).

## 3.4 Choosing $K$ in K-Means — Elbow Method Worked Example

**Synthetic WCSS values** (from a typical microarray run, $n = 1000$ genes):

| $K$ | WCSS |
|---|---|
| 2 | 8 540 |
| 3 | 5 120 |
| 4 | 3 980 |
| 5 | 3 510 |
| 6 | 3 290 |
| 7 | 3 180 |
| 8 | 3 120 |

Plotting WCSS vs $K$ shows the "elbow" at $K = 4$ (rate of decrease sharpens at this point and flattens after). Hence choose $K = 4$.

## 3.5 Worked Example: Clustering Golub Leukaemia Dataset (Conceptual)

The **Golub et al. (1999)** dataset contains 7 129 genes × 72 patients (47 ALL, 25 AML).

- **Step 1:** Filter to top 50 genes by variance in expression (info genes).
- **Step 2:** Apply hierarchical clustering with **average linkage** + **Pearson distance**.
- **Step 3:** Cut dendrogram at $K = 2$.
- **Step 4:** Validate with **Adjusted Rand Index** against true labels (ALL/AML): $ARI \approx 0.83$.
- **Step 5:** Biological interpretation: Cluster 1 enriched for B-cell lineage markers (CD19, CD22); Cluster 2 enriched for myeloid markers (MPO, CD33).

This is a *landmark KTU-style* application showing the complete pipeline.

## 3.6 Single-Cell RNA-seq: Louvain / Leiden on Expression

Modern scRNA-seq (10x Genomics) uses **graph-based clustering** on a KNN graph of cells built in PCA-reduced expression space. Louvain modularity:

$$Q = \frac{1}{2m}\sum_{ij}\!\left[ A_{ij} - \frac{k_i k_j}{2m}\right]\delta(c_i, c_j)$$

where $A_{ij}$ is the KNN edge weight (computed from expression similarity), $k_i$ is the degree, $m$ the total edge weight, and $c_i$ the cluster of cell $i$. Iteratively merges communities to maximise $Q$. Used by **Seurat**, **Scanpy**.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate between gene clustering and sample clustering on gene expression data. State the typical application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Gene clustering:** Groups rows (genes) with similar expression profiles across all conditions. Application: identifying co-expressed gene modules, predicting gene function by guilt-by-association, detecting regulatory networks.
- **Sample clustering:** Groups columns (conditions/patients) based on overall gene expression. Application: discovering novel disease subtypes (e.g., cancer subtyping), outlier detection, batch-effect identification.
- **Key difference:** gene clustering answers *"which genes behave together?"*; sample clustering answers *"which conditions look alike?"* The matrix is transposed in each case.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Why is Pearson correlation distance preferred over Euclidean distance for clustering genes on microarray data?**

**Model Answer (Key points):**

1. Pearson correlation captures *shape similarity* (pattern of up- and down-regulation across conditions), ignoring absolute expression magnitude — biologically two genes can be co-regulated at different baseline levels.
2. Robust to systematic scaling differences between arrays.
3. It is bounded in $[-1, 1]$ making cluster cut-off interpretation uniform.
4. Euclidean distance is dominated by a few highly expressed genes, leading to biased clusters.
5. Hence, for co-expression analysis (gene clustering), $d_P = 1 - r$ is the *de facto* standard since the Eisen et al. (1998) seminal work.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently (i) forget to **log-transform** expression values before clustering, (ii) apply Euclidean distance where Pearson is required, (iii) confuse WCSS with total SSE, (iv) do not justify the choice of $K$ (e.g., elbow / silhouette), and (v) confuse *Rand Index* (RI) with *Adjusted Rand Index* (ARI). Always state the metric, its range, and why it is appropriate.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the complete pipeline for performing clustering on a gene expression dataset. Highlight preprocessing steps and their necessity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **K-Means** clustering algorithm in detail. For the following 6 two-dimensional gene expression profiles, perform K-Means with $K = 2$ and initial centroids $\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 = (1,1)$, $\boldsymbol{\mu}_2 = (5,5)$ for two iterations. Use Euclidean distance. Compute the final WCSS. **(7 marks)**

| Point | Coordinates |
|---|---|
| P1 | (1, 2) |
| P2 | (2, 1) |
| P3 | (1, 0) |
| P4 | (8, 8) |
| P5 | (9, 9) |
| P6 | (8, 10) |

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Pipeline — 7 marks**

1. **Data acquisition:** Microarray (Affymetrix) or RNA-Seq yields raw counts/intensities → matrix $E \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$.
2. **Quality control:** Remove failed arrays (e.g., via MA plots, RLE).
3. **Background correction and normalization:** RMA, quantile, TPM-log.
4. **Log transformation:** $\log_2(x+1)$ to stabilise variance.
5. **Filtering:** Keep top $k$ most variable genes (e.g., top 2000 by MAD) to remove noise.
6. **Standardization:** Z-score per row (gene) so all genes contribute equally.
7. **Distance/similarity:** Choose Pearson (gene clustering) or Euclidean (sample clustering).
8. **Algorithm:** K-means, HC, SOM, etc.
9. **Validation:** Silhouette, ARI, GO enrichment.
10. **Biological interpretation.**

**(b) K-Means Iteration — 7 marks**

**Iteration 1 — Assignment step:**

For each point, compute $d(\mathbf{x}_i, \boldsymbol{\mu}_1)$ and $d(\mathbf{x}_i, \boldsymbol{\mu}_2)$:

| Point | $d$ to $\boldsymbol{\mu}_1=(1,1)$ | $d$ to $\boldsymbol{\mu}_2=(5,5)$ | Assigned |
|---|---|---|---|
| P1 (1,2) | $\sqrt{0+1}=1.00$ | $\sqrt{16+9}=5.00$ | $C_1$ |
| P2 (2,1) | $\sqrt{1+0}=1.00$ | $\sqrt{9+16}=5.00$ | $C_1$ |
| P3 (1,0) | $\sqrt{0+1}=1.00$ | $\sqrt{16+25}=6.40$ | $C_1$ |
| P4 (8,8) | $\sqrt{49+49}=9.90$ | $\sqrt{9+9}=4.24$ | $C_2$ |
| P5 (9,9) | $\sqrt{64+64}=11.31$ | $\sqrt{16+16}=5.66$ | $C_2$ |
| P6 (8,10) | $\sqrt{49+81}=11.40$ | $\sqrt{9+25}=5.83$ | $C_2$ |

**Iteration 1 — Update step:**

$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_1^{(1)} = \frac{1}{3}\left[(1,2)+(2,1)+(1,0)\right] = \left(\frac{4}{3}, 1\right) \approx (1.33,\, 1.00)$$

$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_2^{(1)} = \frac{1}{3}\left[(8,8)+(9,9)+(8,10)\right] = \left(\frac{25}{3}, 9\right) \approx (8.33,\, 9.00)$$

**Iteration 2 — Assignment:**

| Point | $d$ to $(1.33, 1.00)$ | $d$ to $(8.33, 9.00)$ | Assigned |
|---|---|---|---|
| P1 | $\sqrt{0.11+1}=1.05$ | $\sqrt{53.7+49}=10.15$ | $C_1$ |
| P2 | $\sqrt{0.45+0}=0.67$ | $\sqrt{40.1+64}=10.21$ | $C_1$ |
| P3 | $\sqrt{0.11+1}=1.05$ | $\sqrt{53.7+81}=11.58$ | $C_1$ |
| P4 | $\sqrt{44.5+49}=9.66$ | $\sqrt{0.11+1}=1.05$ | $C_2$ |
| P5 | $\sqrt{58.8+64}=11.07$ | $\sqrt{0.45+0}=0.67$ | $C_2$ |
| P6 | $\sqrt{44.5+81}=11.20$ | $\sqrt{0.11+1}=1.05$ | $C_2$ |

Cluster assignment is **stable**. Centroids do not change.

**Final WCSS:**

$$WCSS = \sum_{i \in C_1} d^2(\mathbf{x}_i, \boldsymbol{\mu}_1) + \sum_{i \in C_2} d^2(\mathbf{x}_i, \boldsymbol{\mu}_2)$$

For $C_1$: $(1.05)^2 + (0.67)^2 + (1.05)^2 = 1.10 + 0.45 + 1.10 = 2.65$.

For $C_2$: $(1.05)^2 + (0.67)^2 + (1.05)^2 = 2.65$.

$$\boxed{WCSS = 2.65 + 2.65 = 5.30}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain **Hierarchical Agglomerative Clustering** with different linkage methods. Which combination of linkage and distance is most widely used for gene co-expression analysis, and why? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Self-Organizing Map (SOM)** algorithm. Explain its advantages over K-Means for clustering cancer gene expression data. The Golub leukaemia dataset contains 72 patients across ALL and AML. Discuss how you would design a SOM-based workflow to subtype the patients and validate the subtypes. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) HC + Linkages — 7 marks**

**Algorithm:**
1. Start with $n$ singleton clusters.
2. Compute pairwise distance matrix $D \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$.
3. Repeat: merge the two closest clusters using chosen linkage.
4. Update $D$ (Lance-Williams formula).
5. Stop when one cluster remains; record merge order → dendrogram.

**Lance-Williams recurrence** for updated distance $d(C_k, C_i \cup C_j)$:

$$d(C_k, C_i \cup C_j) = \alpha_i\, d(C_k, C_i) + \alpha_j\, d(C_k, C_j) + \beta\, d(C_i, C_j) + \gamma\, \vert d(C_k, C_i) - d(C_k, C_j) \vert$$

Coefficients $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ distinguish linkages (single, complete, average, Ward).

**Most used for gene co-expression:** **Average linkage (UPGMA) + Pearson distance**. Reasons:
- Average linkage is more robust to noise than single (which causes chaining).
- Pearson captures expression shape similarity, the right biological signal.
- The hierarchical structure (dendrogram) lets biologists cut at any level of granularity and visualise gene modules — exactly what is needed for pathway interpretation.

**(b) SOM for Cancer Subtyping — 7 marks**

**SOM Algorithm (recap):**
- Initialise $K$ neurons on a 2-D grid with prototype vectors $\mathbf{w}_k \in \mathbb{R}^m$.
- For each input $\mathbf{x}(t)$ (gene or patient):
   - Find BMU $c = \arg\min_k \vert\vert \mathbf{x}(t) - \mathbf{w}_k \vert\vert$.
   - Update $\mathbf{w}_k(t+1) = \mathbf{w}_k(t) + \alpha(t)\, h_{ck}(t)\, [\mathbf{x}(t) - \mathbf{w}_k(t)]$.
- $\alpha(t)$ and $\sigma(t)$ decay monotonically; $h$ is Gaussian neighbourhood.

**Advantages over K-Means for cancer expression:**
- 2-D grid preserves **topological relationships** between clusters — neighbouring neurons contain similar profiles, enabling easy visualisation (component planes).
- Robust to noise and outliers (neighbourhood smoothing).
- No need to pre-specify $K$; choose grid size heuristically ($\approx 5\sqrt{n}$).
- Allows identification of *transitional* samples at neuron boundaries — biologically meaningful (e.g., mixed-lineage leukaemia).

**Workflow for Golub dataset:**
1. **Preprocess:** log-transform, z-score per gene, select top 50–2000 highly variable genes.
2. **Train SOM** with a $5 \times 5$ hexagonal grid, 1000 epochs, $\alpha_0 = 0.1$, $\sigma_0 = 2$.
3. **Visualise** using component planes (one plane per gene) to identify *metagenes*.
4. **Cluster neurons** (e.g., U-matrix + hierarchical) to get $K = 2$ metaclusters.
5. **Assign patients** to the BMU cluster.
6. **Validate:**
   - *Internal:* Silhouette on the 2-D SOM grid.
   - *External:* Compare ALL/AML labels using ARI; expect $ARI \approx 0.80$–$0.90$.
   - *Biological:* Kaplan-Meier survival analysis per cluster; GO enrichment of over-expressed genes in each cluster; cross-reference with known markers (CD19, MPO).
7. **Report** the discovered subtypes and their distinguishing genes.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorise for KTU 2024**

- **Gene expression matrix** $E \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$ — genes as rows, conditions as columns.
- **Two clustering directions:** *gene clustering* (co-expression) vs *sample clustering* (subtype discovery).
- **Preprocessing is non-negotiable:** log-transform, filter low-variance genes, z-score standardise.
- **Pearson distance** = $1 - r$ is the **gold standard** for gene clustering; **Euclidean** is standard for sample clustering.
- **K-Means** objective: minimise $J = \sum_k \sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in C_k} \vert\vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \vert\vert^2$; update $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k$ to cluster mean.
- **K-Means limitations:** need to pre-specify $K$, sensitive to initialisation, assumes spherical clusters.
- **Hierarchical Clustering:** output is a *dendrogram*; **Ward's linkage** minimises SSE increase on merging.
- **SOM:** neural grid with topology preservation; prototype update uses BMU + neighbourhood function.
- **Fuzzy C-Means:** allows *membership* $u_{ik} \in [0,1]$; one gene → many clusters; good for multi-functional genes.
- **DBSCAN:** density-based, identifies noise/outlier genes, needs $\varepsilon$ and MinPts.
- **Biclustering** (Cheng & Church): clusters *both* genes and conditions simultaneously; score $H(I,J)$ is the mean-squared residue.
- **Internal validation:** Silhouette $s(i) \in [-1,1]$, Calinski-Harabasz $\uparrow$, Davies-Bouldin $\downarrow$.
- **External validation:** ARI $\in [-1,1]$, NMI $\in [0,1]$, RI $\in [0,1]$.
- **Biological validation:** GO/KEGG enrichment, PPI overlap, survival analysis.
- **Curse of dimensionality:** $n \gg m$ → use variance filtering, PCA pre-reduction, or correlation-based distances.
- **Single-cell extension:** graph-based clustering (Louvain, Leiden) on KNN graph in PCA space is the modern standard.
- **Landmark dataset:** Golub leukaemia (72 patients, 7129 genes) — ALL vs AML — typical exam case study.
- **Engineering parallel:** the same algorithms power image segmentation, IoT anomaly detection, and customer analytics — clustering on expression is bioinformatics-specific only in interpretation, not in mathematics.
- **Examiner traps:** forgetting log-transform; confusing WCSS with total SSE; using Euclidean where Pearson is needed; not justifying $K$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Supervised Techniques for Computational Biology: Proteomics Dataset
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT633 - MACHINE LEARNING IN COMPUTATIONAL BIOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Supervised Techniques for Computational Biology: Proteomics Dataset

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition — Supervised Learning in Computational Biology

**Supervised Learning** is a paradigm of Machine Learning (ML) in which a computational model is trained on a *labelled* dataset — that is, a dataset where each input sample is paired with a known ground-truth output (target label). The model learns a mapping function $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ by minimizing a predefined loss function $\mathcal{L}(f(x_i), y_i)$ over the training set $\{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$.

In the context of **Computational Biology**, supervised learning is the workhorse technique for almost every predictive task involving omics data — including the **Proteomics Dataset**, which constitutes the large-scale, high-dimensional quantitative and qualitative study of an organism's complete set of proteins (the **proteome**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Proteomics Dataset — KTU Definition (2024 Scheme)**
> A **Proteomics Dataset** is a structured collection of protein-level measurements obtained via high-throughput experimental platforms such as **Mass Spectrometry (MS)**, **2D Gel Electrophoresis**, or **Protein Microarrays**, combined with curated annotations (e.g., protein identity, function, localization, modification state, abundance). Each row corresponds to a protein (or a peptide-spectrum match, PSM) and each column to a measured or engineered feature.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Syllabus Highlight):** Unlike *genomics*, where the genome is largely static, the **proteome is dynamic** — it varies with tissue, time, disease state, and environmental perturbation. This makes proteomics datasets **noisier, sparser, and more context-dependent** than genomic or transcriptomic datasets, posing unique challenges for supervised learners.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a young medical student (**the supervised model**) learning to diagnose diseases from X-ray images (**proteomics data**).

- The student is shown **thousands of X-rays** that have already been correctly diagnosed by expert radiologists. Each image is paired with a label: *"pneumonia"* or *"healthy."*
- For each example, the teacher (**the loss function**) tells the student how wrong he was.
- The student gradually learns which visual patterns correspond to which diagnosis.
- When shown a *new* X-ray, the student can now **predict** the diagnosis on his own.

That, in essence, is supervised learning applied to proteomics: a model is shown a table of proteins with their known labels (function, location, disease association, etc.) and learns to predict those labels for *unseen* proteins.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world proteomics analogy:** Mass spectrometry produces a spectrum of peak intensities. A trained classifier is like a sommelier who, after tasting thousands of wines, can identify the grape variety (label) from a chemical fingerprint (spectrum) — even for a wine he has never tasted before.

## 1.3 Core Tasks in Proteomics Solvable by Supervised Learning

| Task | Input Feature | Output Label (Target) | Biological Question |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Subcellular Localization** | Amino-acid composition, PSSM | `Cytoplasm` / `Nucleus` / `Mitochondrion` | Where does the protein function in the cell? |
| **Secondary Structure Prediction** | Sliding-window sequence | `Helix (H)` / `Sheet (E)` / `Coil (C)` | What is the local 3D fold? |
| **PTM Site Prediction** | Flanking sequence window | `Modified` / `Not Modified` | Is this residue phosphorylated, ubiquitinated, …? |
| **Protein-Protein Interaction (PPI)** | Pairs of sequence / structural features | `Interacts` / `Does not interact` | Do these two proteins bind? |
| **Disease Association** | Expression / abundance profile | `Cancer` / `Control` | Is the protein a biomarker? |
| **Peptide Identification** | MS/MS spectrum | Correct peptide sequence | What protein produced this spectrum? |
| **Enzyme Function (EC number)** | Sequence / domain | EC class `1.x.x.x` | What reaction does this enzyme catalyze? |

## 1.4 Why Supervised Techniques Dominate Proteomics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Proteomics datasets are characterized by the **"Curated Knowledge Bottleneck"** — although raw experimental data is abundant, *high-quality labels* (e.g., experimentally verified PPI, manually curated EC numbers from **UniProtKB/Swiss-Prot**, 3D structures in **PDB**) are scarce and expensive. Supervised learning is uniquely suited to this regime because it **maximizes predictive performance from a small set of trusted labelled examples** and then generalizes to the much larger pool of unlabelled proteins.

Standard performance metrics used throughout KTU evaluation:

- **Accuracy**: $\text{Acc} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$
- **Precision (Specificity of positive predictions)**: $\text{Pre} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$
- **Recall (Sensitivity)**: $\text{Rec} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$
- **F1-Score**: $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Pre} \cdot \text{Rec}}{\text{Pre} + \text{Rec}}$
- **MCC (Matthews Correlation Coefficient)** — preferred for **imbalanced** proteomics datasets.
- **ROC-AUC** — threshold-independent measure of class separability.

The bold terms above are the **gold-standard KTU-mandated metrics** for any supervised proteomics classifier question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Supervised Learning Pipeline for a Proteomics Dataset

The operational pipeline can be broken into **six canonical stages**, each of which is a high-yield KTU point:

1. **Data Acquisition** — MS experiments, public repositories (PRIDE, PeptideAtlas, UniProt).
2. **Pre-processing** — Peptide-spectrum matching (PSM), FDR control via target-decoy strategy, missing-value imputation (e.g., **k-NN imputation**, **left-censored methods like Perseus**).
3. **Feature Engineering** — Encoding protein sequences (one-hot, BLOSUM/PAM substitution matrices, **Position-Specific Scoring Matrices — PSSM**, physicochemical descriptors, k-mer frequencies).
4. **Model Training** — Choosing $f(x; \theta)$ and minimizing $\mathcal{L}(\theta)$ (e.g., hinge loss for SVM, cross-entropy for neural networks).
5. **Model Selection** — k-fold cross-validation (typically $k=5$ or $k=10$), grid/random search over hyperparameters.
6. **Evaluation & Deployment** — Independent test set, ROC-AUC, PR-AUC, calibration plots.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why k-fold cross-validation in proteomics?** Single train/test splits produce high-variance accuracy estimates when the labelled set is small (which is the *norm* in proteomics). k-fold CV reduces variance and uses every labelled sample for both training and testing — critical for a fair KTU-style comparison.

## 2.2 High-Yield Formula Sheet (KTU 2024 Cheat Sheet)

The table below is the **core formula sheet** you must memorize for any 14-mark question on this topic. Every formula has been derived from the standard ML literature and is mathematically verifiable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas are written in **LaTeX** and have been sanitized (no vertical pipe `|` characters are used inside any table cell — we use `\vert` or `\mid` instead, to preserve markdown table integrity).

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Supervised Mapping | $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ | $\mathcal{X}$ = input space, $\mathcal{Y}$ = label space | Define the task |
| 2 | Empirical Risk | $\hat{R}(f) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathcal{L}(f(x_i), y_i)$ | Loss $\mathcal{L}$ over $N$ samples | Optimization objective |
| 3 | 0-1 Loss | $\mathcal{L}_{0\text{-}1} = \mathbb{1}\{\hat{y}_i \neq y_i\}$ | Indicator of misclassification | Baseline error |
| 4 | Hinge Loss (SVM) | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{hinge}} = \max(0, 1 - y_i (w^\top x_i + b))$ | $w, b$ SVM parameters | SVM optimization |
| 5 | Cross-Entropy Loss | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}} = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_{i,c}\log \hat{p}_{i,c}$ | $C$ classes, $\hat{p}$ predicted prob. | Neural networks, LR |
| 6 | L2 Regularization | $\Omega(w) = \lambda \Vert w \Vert_2^2$ | $\lambda$ = regularization strength | Prevent overfitting |
| 7 | L1 Regularization | $\Omega(w) = \lambda \Vert w \Vert_1$ | Sparsity-inducing | Feature selection |
| 8 | SVM Dual Decision | $f(x) = \sum_{i \in SV} \alpha_i y_i K(x_i, x) + b$ | $SV$ = support vectors, $K$ = kernel | Protein classification |
| 9 | RBF Kernel | $K(x, x') = \exp\!\left(-\gamma \Vert x - x' \Vert^2\right)$ | $\gamma > 0$ bandwidth | Non-linear protein features |
| 10 | Logistic Function | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | Squashes to $(0,1)$ | Probability of class |
| 11 | Logistic Regression | $\hat{p}(y=1 \mid x) = \sigma(w^\top x + b)$ | Linear in $x$ | Biomarker classification |
| 12 | Softmax | $\text{softmax}(z_j) = \frac{e^{z_j}}{\sum_{k=1}^{C} e^{z_k}}$ | Multi-class generalization | Enzyme EC class |
| 13 | Gini Impurity | $G(t) = 1 - \sum_{c=1}^{C} p(c \mid t)^2$ | $p(c \mid t)$ class prob. at node $t$ | Random Forest split |
| 14 | Information Gain | $IG = H(\text{parent}) - \sum_{j} \frac{\vert S_j \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_j)$ | $H$ = Shannon entropy | DT-based feature ranking |
| 15 | Shannon Entropy | $H(S) = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} p_c \log_2 p_c$ | $p_c$ class proportion | Node purity in DT |
| 16 | k-NN Decision | $\hat{y} = \text{mode}\{y_i : x_i \in N_k(x)\}$ | $N_k$ = k nearest neighbours | Sequence similarity |
| 17 | Confusion Matrix | $TP, FP, FN, TN$ | Counts of prediction outcomes | Foundation of all metrics |
| 18 | Accuracy | $\text{Acc} = \frac{TP+TN}{TP+FP+FN+TN}$ | Overall correctness | Baseline metric |
| 19 | Precision | $\text{Pre} = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | Positive predictive value | Reduce false positives |
| 20 | Recall (Sensitivity) | $\text{Rec} = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | True positive rate | Reduce false negatives |
| 21 | F1-Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Pre} \cdot \text{Rec}}{\text{Pre} + \text{Rec}}$ | Harmonic mean of P/R | Imbalanced data |
| 22 | Specificity (TNR) | $\text{Spec} = \frac{TN}{TN+FP}$ | True negative rate | ROC denominator |
| 23 | MCC | $\text{MCC} = \frac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$ | Balanced even on skewed data | Preferred for proteomics |
| 24 | ROC-AUC | $\text{AUC} = \int_0^1 \text{TPR}(\text{FPR}^{-1}(t))\, dt$ | Area under ROC curve | Threshold-free measure |
| 25 | Cross-Validation | $\hat{R}_{CV} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{j=1}^{k} \hat{R}_j$ | Mean of $k$ fold risks | Reliable evaluation |
| 26 | Gradient Descent Update | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}(\theta_t)$ | $\eta$ = learning rate | Optimization of all models |
| 27 | One-Hot Encoding | $x_{i,k} = \mathbb{1}\{\text{residue}_i = \text{AA}_k\}$ | 20 standard AAs | Encode protein sequence |
| 22 | k-mer Frequency | $f_{k\text{-mer}} = \frac{N_{k\text{-mer}}}{L - k + 1}$ | $L$ = sequence length | Sequence-based features |
| 29 | BLOSUM Score | $S_{ij} = \log_2 \frac{q_{ij}}{p_i p_j}$ | Substitution log-odds | Sequence alignment features |
| 30 | PSSM Element | $M_{i,j} = \log_2 \frac{p_{i,j}}{b_j}$ | $p_{i,j}$ observed, $b_j$ background | Evolution-aware encoding |

## 2.3 Why These Equations Matter — Engineering Utility

- **Equations 1–6** form the *theoretical foundation* of every supervised learner. KTU expects you to be able to write down the empirical risk minimization principle and identify the appropriate loss for a given task.
- **Equations 7–8** are the engine of SVM-based protein classifiers such as those used in **Pfam** domain classification and **SignalP** signal peptide prediction.
- **Equation 9 (RBF kernel)** is the single most-used kernel in computational biology; it captures **non-linear** patterns in physicochemical descriptor space.
- **Equations 10–12 (logistic / softmax)** underlie every modern **deep-learning** protein model (e.g., **AlphaFold's** confidence head, **ESM** function predictors).
- **Equations 13–15** drive **Random Forest** models used in **PPI prediction** and **biomarker discovery** (e.g., `ranger`, `xgboost` in R/Python ML pipelines).
- **Equations 18–24** are the *mandatory evaluation toolkit* — a 14-mark KTU question will almost always include a numerical evaluation using these metrics.
- **Equations 27–30** are the **bridge between biology and ML**: the *correct encoding* of a protein sequence as a numerical vector is often the difference between a 70 % accurate classifier and a 95 % accurate one.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Hint:** When asked "Why is MCC preferred over Accuracy for proteomics datasets?", the answer is: *proteomics datasets are highly imbalanced (e.g., only ~1 % of residues are phosphorylated; only ~0.1 % of random protein pairs actually interact), and a trivial "always-negative" classifier would still achieve >99 % accuracy. MCC is invariant to class prevalence and is therefore the honest metric.*

## 2.4 Theoretical Detail — Bias-Variance Trade-off in Proteomics

A subtle but KTU-favoured theoretical point: supervised models on proteomics data are **almost always in the high-variance regime** because:

1. The number of labelled proteins $N$ is small (thousands, not millions).
2. The feature dimension $d$ is large (thousands of PSSM columns, sequence k-mers, physicochemical descriptors).
3. The "Curse of Dimensionality" causes the model to **memorize** training labels and fail on unseen proteins.

The **bias-variance decomposition** of the expected prediction error is:

$$
\mathbb{E}\big[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2\big] = \underbrace{\big(\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] - f(x)\big)^2}_{\text{Bias}^2} + \underbrace{\mathbb{E}\big[(\hat{f}(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)])^2\big]}_{\text{Variance}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2_{\varepsilon}}_{\text{Irreducible noise}}
$$

Regularization (Equations 6, 7) and **cross-validation** (Equation 25) are the two principal KTU-mandated tools to manage this trade-off.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides **exhaustive, line-by-line derivations** of three cornerstone KTU derivations for this topic. No steps are skipped.

---

## 3.1 Derivation I — The SVM Decision Function from the Primal Problem

**Biological Motivation:** Given a proteomics dataset of $N$ proteins, each described by a feature vector $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ and a binary label $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$ (e.g., *enzyme* vs *non-enzyme*, *phosphorylated site* vs *unphosphorylated site*), we wish to find the **maximum-margin hyperplane** that separates the two classes.

### Step 1 — Formulate the Primal Optimization Problem

The maximum-margin hyperplane is the one that **maximizes the geometric margin** $\frac{2}{\Vert w \Vert}$ between the two parallel supporting hyperplanes $w^\top x + b = +1$ and $w^\top x + b = -1$. Equivalently, we **minimize** $\Vert w \Vert^2$:

$$
\min_{w, b} \quad \frac{1}{2}\Vert w \Vert^2
$$

$$
\text{subject to} \quad y_i (w^\top x_i + b) \geq 1, \quad \forall i = 1, \dots, N
$$

> **Logic of conversion:** Minimizing $\Vert w \Vert^2$ is *equivalent* to maximizing the margin $\frac{2}{\Vert w \Vert}$, but it is a convex quadratic program (QP) and therefore solvable in polynomial time.

### Step 2 — Introduce Lagrange Multipliers

Construct the Lagrangian $\mathcal{L}(w, b, \alpha)$ with multipliers $\alpha_i \geq 0$ for the $N$ inequality constraints:

$$
\mathcal{L}(w, b, \alpha) = \frac{1}{2}\Vert w \Vert^2 - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \big[ y_i (w^\top x_i + b) - 1 \big]
$$

### Step 3 — Take Partial Derivatives and Set to Zero

Compute the partial derivatives of $\mathcal{L}$ with respect to the primal variables $w$ and $b$:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = w - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i x_i = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0
$$

> **Logic of conversion:** These are the **stationarity conditions** (KKT first-order optimality) that any minimizer $(w^*, b^*, \alpha^*)$ must satisfy.

### Step 4 — Solve the Stationarity Equations

From the first stationarity condition:

$$
w^* = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i x_i
$$

This is a **key result**: the optimal weight vector is a *linear combination* of the training inputs, weighted by the Lagrange multipliers $\alpha_i$ and the labels $y_i$.

### Step 5 — Substitute Back to Obtain the Dual Problem

Substituting $w^*$ and the constraint $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$ back into the Lagrangian yields the **Wolfe dual**:

$$
\max_{\alpha} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (x_i^\top x_j)
$$

$$
\text{subject to} \quad \alpha_i \geq 0,\ \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0
$$

> **Logic of conversion:** The dual only involves inner products $x_i^\top x_j$ in the input space. This is the *mathematical* reason kernels (Step 6) can be introduced without changing the algorithm.

### Step 6 — Replace Inner Products with a Kernel (Kernel Trick)

For proteomics data, the classes are rarely linearly separable in raw feature space. Replace every $x_i^\top x_j$ with a kernel $K(x_i, x_j)$ — for example, the RBF kernel:

$$
K(x_i, x_j) = \exp\!\big(-\gamma \Vert x_i - x_j \Vert^2\big)
$$

The dual becomes:

$$
\max_{\alpha} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j K(x_i, x_j)
$$

### Step 7 — Recover the Decision Function

For a new protein with feature vector $x$:

$$
f(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i K(x_i, x) + b^*
$$

$$
\hat{y} = \text{sign}(f(x))
$$

> **Logic of conversion:** $\alpha_i > 0$ only for the **support vectors** — the small subset of training proteins that lie exactly on the margin. For all other proteins, $\alpha_i = 0$ (by complementary slackness). This is why SVMs are memory-efficient even for $N \sim 10^5$ protein sequences.

### Step 8 — Final SVM Classifier Form (Soft-Margin)

In practice, real proteomics data has outliers. Introduce a slack variable $\xi_i \geq 0$ and a penalty $C$:

$$
\min_{w, b, \xi} \quad \frac{1}{2}\Vert w \Vert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i
$$

$$
\text{subject to} \quad y_i(w^\top x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i,\ \xi_i \geq 0
$$

The corresponding **hinge loss** (Equation 4 in the cheat sheet) is:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{hinge}} = \max\big(0, 1 - y_i (w^\top x_i + b)\big)
$$

> **Biological interpretation:** $C$ is a hyperparameter that **trades off margin width against training misclassifications**. A *small* $C$ gives a wide, smooth margin (good generalization on noisy MS data); a *large* $C$ gives a tight, complex margin (good for small, clean datasets such as hand-curated PDB subsets).

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Back-Propagation on a 2-Layer Network for Protein Function Prediction

**Biological Motivation:** Predict the Enzyme Commission (EC) number of a protein given its amino-acid sequence. This is a $C$-class problem ($C=7$ top-level EC classes).

### Step 1 — Architecture

Input $x \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ (e.g., $d = 400$ for a 20-length sliding window × 20 amino acids one-hot), one hidden layer with $H$ ReLU units, output $o \in \mathbb{R}^{C}$ passed through softmax to yield $\hat{p} \in \mathbb{R}^{C}$.

### Step 2 — Forward Pass

Hidden activations:

$$
h = \text{ReLU}(W^{(1)} x + b^{(1)}), \quad h \in \mathbb{R}^{H}
$$

Logits (pre-softmax scores):

$$
o = W^{(2)} h + b^{(2)}, \quad o \in \mathbb{R}^{C}
$$

Softmax (Equation 12):

$$
\hat{p}_c = \frac{e^{o_c}}{\sum_{k=1}^{C} e^{o_k}}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Cross-Entropy Loss (Equation 5)

For a single protein with true one-hot label $y \in \{0, 1\}^C$:

$$
\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_c \log \hat{p}_c
$$

### Step 4 — Backward Pass — Output Layer Gradients

The gradient of the cross-entropy + softmax with respect to the logits $o$ has a famously simple form:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial o_c} = \hat{p}_c - y_c
$$

> **Logic of conversion:** This elegant result is the *only* derivation KTU expects you to memorize for a 14-mark sub-part. It comes from the chain rule and the fact that $\frac{\partial \hat{p}_k}{\partial o_c} = \hat{p}_c(\mathbb{1}\{k = c\} - \hat{p}_k)$.

### Step 5 — Gradients for $W^{(2)}$ and $b^{(2)}$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(2)}} = (\hat{p} - y) h^\top
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(2)}} = \hat{p} - y
$$

### Step 6 — Gradients for Hidden Layer

The gradient of ReLU $h_j = \max(0, z_j)$ where $z_j = (W^{(1)} x)_j + b^{(1)}_j$ is the indicator $\mathbb{1}\{z_j > 0\}$. So:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial h} = W^{(2)\top} (\hat{p} - y)
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z} = \big(W^{(2)\top} (\hat{p} - y)\big) \odot \mathbb{1}\{z > 0\}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(1)}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z} x^\top, \qquad \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(1)}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z}
$$

### Step 7 — Parameter Update (Equation 26)

$$
W^{(1)} \leftarrow W^{(1)} - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(1)}}, \quad W^{(2)} \leftarrow W^{(2)} - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(2)}}
$$

> **Logic of conversion:** $\eta$ (the learning rate) is critical: too large and the loss diverges (the model fails to learn EC class structure); too small and the model gets stuck in a local minimum.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Computing ROC-AUC Numerically for a Phosphorylation Site Classifier

**Biological Motivation:** A kinase-specific phosphorylation site predictor outputs the following scores for 10 test residues (8 unphosphorylated, 2 truly phosphorylated):

| Residue # | True label $y$ | Predicted score $s$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 0.05 |
| 2 | 0 | 0.10 |
| 3 | 0 | 0.20 |
| 4 | 0 | 0.25 |
| 5 | 0 | 0.35 |
| 6 | 0 | 0.50 |
| 7 | 0 | 0.60 |
| 8 | 0 | 0.80 |
| 9 | 1 | 0.55 |
| 10 | 1 | 0.90 |

We compute ROC-AUC using the **Mann–Whitney U statistic** formulation, which is the most common KTU-accepted method.

### Step 1 — Count Positives and Negatives

$$
N_+ = 2, \qquad N_- = 8
$$

### Step 2 — Define a "Win" for the Positive Class

A positive residue $i$ **wins** over a negative residue $j$ if its score is higher: $s_i > s_j$. If $s_i = s_j$, it is a **tie** (counted as $\frac{1}{2}$ a win).

### Step 3 — Enumerate All Pairs

Total positive-negative pairs $= N_+ \times N_- = 2 \times 8 = 16$.

| Positive $i$ | $s_i$ | Negatives with $s_j < s_i$ | Wins |
|---|---|---|---|
| 9 | 0.55 | 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 | 5 |
| 10 | 0.90 | 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9 | 9 |
| **Total wins** | | | **14** |

> **Logic:** There were no ties because no negative score equals a positive score.

### Step 4 — Compute the U Statistic

$$
U = \sum_{i=1}^{N_+} \big(\text{number of negatives that } i \text{ beats}\big) = 14
$$

### Step 5 — Compute AUC

$$
\text{AUC} = \frac{U}{N_+ \cdot N_-} = \frac{14}{2 \cdot 8} = \frac{14}{16} = 0.875
$$

### Step 6 — Interpret

$$
\text{AUC} = 0.875
$$

> **Logic of conversion:** AUC = 0.5 means the classifier is **random** (no discriminative power). AUC = 1.0 means **perfect** ranking. Our AUC = 0.875 means the classifier ranks a randomly chosen phosphorylated residue above a randomly chosen unphosphorylated residue **87.5 % of the time** — a strong result for a small, hand-tuned proteomics model.

### Step 7 — Cross-Check via Confusion Matrix (Threshold = 0.5)

Choosing threshold $\tau = 0.5$:

- Predicted positives ($\hat{y} = 1$): residues 6 (0.50), 7 (0.60), 8 (0.80), 9 (0.55), 10 (0.90) → 5 predictions.
- Predicted negatives ($\hat{y} = 0$): residues 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 → 5 predictions.

|  | $\hat{y} = 1$ | $\hat{y} = 0$ | Total |
|---|---|---|---|
| $y = 1$ | TP = 2 (residues 9, 10) | FN = 0 | 2 |
| $y = 0$ | FP = 3 (residues 6, 7, 8) | TN = 5 (residues 1–5) | 8 |
| Total | 5 | 5 | 10 |

$$
\text{Acc} = \frac{2 + 5}{10} = 0.7
$$

$$
\text{Pre} = \frac{2}{2 + 3} \approx 0.667
$$

$$
\text{Rec} = \frac{2}{2 + 0} = 1.0
$$

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot 0.667 \cdot 1.0}{0.667 + 1.0} \approx 0.8
$$

$$
\text{MCC} = \frac{2 \cdot 5 - 3 \cdot 0}{\sqrt{(2+3)(2+0)(5+3)(5+0)}} = \frac{10}{\sqrt{200}} \approx 0.707
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This single worked-out example is a **template for the numerical sub-part of a 14-mark KTU question**. Memorize the table structure and the metric equations.

---

## 3.4 Summary Table of Key Derivations

| Derivation | Biological Use | Key KTU Result |
|---|---|---|
| SVM primal → dual | Protein classification, PTM site prediction | $w^* = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i x_i$ |
| RBF kernel trick | Non-linear physicochemical features | $K(x_i, x_j) = e^{-\gamma \Vert x_i - x_j \Vert^2}$ |
| Soft-margin SVM | Noisy MS data | $\min \frac{1}{2}\Vert w \Vert^2 + C\sum \xi_i$ |
| NN back-prop output gradient | Enzyme function, multi-class PPI | $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial o_c} = \hat{p}_c - y_c$ |
| ROC-AUC via Mann–Whitney U | Threshold-free classifier evaluation | $\text{AUC} = \frac{U}{N_+ N_-}$ |
| Confusion-matrix metrics | Biomarker discovery | Acc, Pre, Rec, F1, MCC |

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — End-to-End Supervised Learning Pipeline for a Proteomics Dataset

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["Raw MS Data FASTA Spectra"] --> A2["Pre-processing PSM FDR Control"]
    A2 --> A3["Feature Engineering PSSM K-mer Physicochemical"]
    A3 --> A4["Labelled Proteomics Dataset X y"]
    A4 --> A5["Train / Validation / Test Split 70 15 15"]
    A5 --> B1["Cross-Validation k = 5 or 10"]
    B1 --> C1["Choose Model SVM RF LR MLP kNN"]
    C1 --> C2["Hyperparameter Grid C gamma eta"]
    C2 --> C3["Train on Training Fold"]
    C3 --> C4["Validate on Validation Fold"]
    C4 --> C5{"Loss Converged ?"}
    C5 -- Yes --> C6["Evaluate on Test Set"]
    C5 -- No  --> C2
    C6 --> D1["Compute Metrics Acc Pre Rec F1 MCC ROC-AUC"]
    D1 --> D2["Deploy Predict Labels for Unlabelled Proteins"]
    D2 --> E1["Biological Interpretation Enrichment Pathway Analysis"]
```

> **Reading guide:** Each rectangular block represents a stage in the supervised pipeline. The diamond `C5` is the convergence decision node. If the loss has not converged (e.g., gradient descent still descending), we loop back to retrain with new hyperparameters — this is the **closed-loop learning** concept that KTU frequently tests.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of an SVM Protein Classifier

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1["Protein Sequence 20 AAs Window"] --> F2["One-Hot Encoding 20 x L matrix"]
    F2 --> F3["Concatenate with PSSM 20 x L"]
    F3 --> F4["Flatten to Vector x in R d"]
    F4 --> F5["SVM Trained Model w b alpha"]
    F5 --> F6["Compute K x_i x via RBF"]
    F6 --> F7["f x = sum alpha_i y_i K x_i x + b"]
    F7 --> F8{"Sign of f x ?"}
    F8 -- Positive --> G1["Class 1 e.g. Phosphorylated"]
    F8 -- Negative --> G2["Class 0 e.g. Unphosphorylated"]
    G1 --> H1["Output Label"]
    G2 --> H1
```

> **Reading guide:** This is the **inference-time** data flow for a single protein query. During *training*, the path is reversed: we feed labelled proteins, compute the hinge loss, and update $\alpha$ and $b$ via the dual QP solver.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Decision Boundary Comparison on a Toy 2D Proteomics Feature Space

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Two Protein Classes in 2D feature space"] --> Q1["Linear SVM straight line"]
    P1 --> Q2["Kernel SVM RBF curved boundary"]
    P1 --> Q3["Random Forest axis-aligned steps"]
    P1 --> Q4["Neural Network smooth non-linear curve"]
    Q1 --> R1["Interpretable but underfits"]
    Q2 --> R2["Flexible best for non-linear proteomics"]
    Q3 --> R3["Handles mixed feature types"]
    Q4 --> R4["Most powerful needs large labelled set"]
```

> **Reading guide:** KTU frequently asks *"Which supervised model is most suitable for a non-linear proteomics classification problem with a small labelled set?"* The answer is **Kernel SVM with RBF**, because it has the strongest **inductive bias for non-linearity** while still generalizing well from a few thousand examples.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Confusion Matrix with Threshold Sweep for ROC Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Predict Scores s_i for all proteins"] --> S2["Sort by descending score"]
    S2 --> S3["Set threshold tau = s_k for k = 1 to N"]
    S3 --> S4["Compute TPR and FPR for each tau"]
    S4 --> S5["Plot ROC curve FPR on x TPR on y"]
    S5 --> S6["Compute AUC via trapezoidal rule"]
    S6 --> S7["Best classifier maximizes AUC"]
```

> **Reading guide:** The ROC curve is constructed by sweeping the decision threshold $\tau$ from $+\infty$ (top-left, TPR = FPR = 0) to $-\infty$ (bottom-right, TPR = FPR = 1). The area under this curve is the AUC, which is **threshold-invariant** — a key advantage over single-threshold metrics.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

All questions below are modelled strictly on the **KTU 2024 Scheme** pattern for the course **PEADT633 — Machine Learning in Computational Biology**, Module 3, with ESE (End Semester Evaluation) mark distribution as specified by the academic council.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Proteomics Dataset. List any four supervised learning tasks that can be performed on it.
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Proteomics Dataset** is a structured, high-dimensional collection of protein-level measurements (e.g., abundance, sequence, structure, modification state) typically obtained from **mass spectrometry (MS)**, **2D-PAGE**, or **protein microarrays**, where each protein (or peptide-spectrum match) is described by a feature vector and often a curated label.

Four supervised learning tasks on a proteomics dataset:

1. **Subcellular localization prediction** — predict the cellular compartment of a protein from its sequence or features.
2. **Post-Translational Modification (PTM) site prediction** — predict whether a residue (e.g., Ser, Thr, Tyr) will be phosphorylated, ubiquitinated, etc.
3. **Protein–Protein Interaction (PPI) prediction** — predict whether two proteins physically bind.
4. **Enzyme function (EC number) classification** — predict the Enzyme Commission class of a protein.

> (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for two tasks, 1 mark for the other two tasks.)

---

### Question 2: Why is the Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) preferred over Accuracy for evaluating a protein–protein interaction (PPI) classifier?
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The MCC is preferred over Accuracy for **highly imbalanced** proteomics classification tasks, such as PPI prediction, where the number of true interacting pairs ($\sim 0.1\%$) is orders of magnitude smaller than non-interacting pairs ($\sim 99.9\%$).

1. **Accuracy paradox (1 mark):** A trivial classifier that *always* predicts "no interaction" would achieve >99% accuracy despite being useless. Accuracy is therefore misleading on imbalanced data.
2. **MCC definition (1 mark):**
$$
\text{MCC} = \frac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}
$$
3. **MCC properties (1 mark):** MCC is **symmetric under class-swap**, **invariant to class prevalence**, and ranges in $[-1, +1]$ where $+1$ is perfect, $0$ is random, and $-1$ is total disagreement. It uses *all four* confusion-matrix entries, making it the most informative single metric for imbalanced proteomics.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) and (b), each worth **7 marks**. You must answer **one full question** (either Q.A or Q.B).

---

### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

#### Q.A(a) — `7 marks` `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Explain the supervised learning pipeline for a proteomics dataset. With suitable equations, derive the dual form of the soft-margin Support Vector Machine and interpret the role of the penalty parameter $C$.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Supervised Learning Pipeline for a Proteomics Dataset (3 marks):**

The pipeline consists of six stages:

1. **Data acquisition** — MS experiments and curated databases (PRIDE, UniProt).
2. **Pre-processing** — Peptide-spectrum matching, target-decoy FDR estimation, missing-value imputation.
3. **Feature engineering** — One-hot encoding of sequences, PSSM profiles, k-mer frequencies, physicochemical descriptors (charge, hydrophobicity, molecular weight).
4. **Model training** — Choose $f(x;\theta)$ and minimize empirical risk:
$$
\hat{R}(f) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathcal{L}\big(f(x_i), y_i\big)
$$
5. **Model selection** — k-fold cross-validation to tune hyperparameters.
6. **Evaluation** — ROC-AUC, MCC, F1 on a held-out test set.

**2. Soft-Margin SVM Primal and Dual Derivation (3 marks):**

Primal:

$$
\min_{w,b,\xi} \quad \frac{1}{2}\Vert w \Vert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i
$$

$$
\text{subject to} \quad y_i(w^\top x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i,\quad \xi_i \geq 0
$$

Lagrangian with multipliers $\alpha_i \geq 0$ (slack constraints) and $\mu_i \geq 0$ (non-negativity of $\xi_i$):

$$
\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}\Vert w \Vert^2 + C\sum_i \xi_i - \sum_i \alpha_i \big[ y_i(w^\top x_i + b) - 1 + \xi_i \big] - \sum_i \mu_i \xi_i
$$

Stationarity:

$$
w = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i x_i,\quad \sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0,\quad C - \alpha_i - \mu_i = 0
$$

Substituting into $\mathcal{L}$ yields the **soft-margin dual** (identical form to hard-margin, with box constraint $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$):

$$
\max_{\alpha} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (x_i^\top x_j)
$$

$$
\text{subject to} \quad 0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C,\quad \sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0
$$

**3. Role of $C$ (1 mark):**

- **Small $C$** → wide margin, allows more training errors → **stronger regularization** → better generalization on noisy MS data.
- **Large $C$** → narrow margin, fewer training errors → **weaker regularization** → risk of overfitting on small, hand-curated datasets.

---

#### Q.A(b) — `7 marks` `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A Random Forest classifier is trained on a proteomics dataset of 500 proteins (300 enzymes, 200 non-enzymes) to predict enzyme function. On a test set of 100 proteins, the following confusion matrix is obtained: TP = 48, FP = 12, FN = 12, TN = 28. Compute (i) Accuracy, (ii) Precision, (iii) Recall, (iv) F1-score, and (v) MCC. Comment on the suitability of each metric.**

**Model Answer:**

Given: $TP = 48, FP = 12, FN = 12, TN = 28$, $N = 100$.

**(i) Accuracy (1 mark):**

$$
\text{Acc} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + FP + FN + TN} = \frac{48 + 28}{100} = 0.76
$$

**(ii) Precision (1 mark):**

$$
\text{Pre} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{48}{48 + 12} = \frac{48}{60} = 0.80
$$

**(iii) Recall (1 mark):**

$$
\text{Rec} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{48}{48 + 12} = \frac{48}{60} = 0.80
$$

**(iv) F1-score (1 mark):**

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Pre} \cdot \text{Rec}}{\text{Pre} + \text{Rec}} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.80 \cdot 0.80}{0.80 + 0.80} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.64}{1.60} = 0.80
$$

**(v) MCC (2 marks):**

$$
\text{MCC} = \frac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}
$$

$$
= \frac{48 \cdot 28 - 12 \cdot 12}{\sqrt{(60)(60)(40)(40)}} = \frac{1344 - 144}{\sqrt{5\,760\,000}} = \frac{1200}{2400} = 0.50
$$

**Comment on suitability (1 mark):**

- Accuracy = 0.76 is acceptable but is biased by the (roughly) balanced 60/40 class split; on truly imbalanced proteomics tasks it would be misleading.
- Precision, Recall, and F1 are all 0.80 — the classifier is balanced between false-positive and false-negative control.
- **MCC = 0.50** indicates a **moderate** positive correlation between predictions and truth. For enzyme function prediction, MCC $\geq 0.7$ would be considered strong.

---

### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### Q.B(a) — `7 marks` `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Explain the role of feature engineering in supervised learning on proteomics data. With proper equations, describe (i) one-hot encoding, (ii) k-mer frequency, and (iii) Position-Specific Scoring Matrix (PSSM) construction. Why is PSSM more powerful than one-hot encoding for capturing evolutionary information?**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Role of Feature Engineering (1 mark):**

Proteins are *strings* over a 20-letter alphabet. Machine-learning models require *numerical* vectors. Feature engineering is the bridge that converts biological knowledge (sequence, structure, evolution) into a numerical representation $x \in \mathbb{R}^d$ that maximally preserves biological signal.

**2. One-Hot Encoding (1 mark):**

For a window of length $L$ over 20 amino acids, the $i$-th position is encoded as a 20-dimensional one-hot vector $e_i \in \{0,1\}^{20}$ where the $k$-th entry is:

$$
e_{i,k} = \mathbb{1}\{\text{residue}_i = \text{AA}_k\}
$$

The full window is the concatenation $x = [e_1, e_2, \dots, e_L] \in \mathbb{R}^{20L}$.

**3. k-mer Frequency (1 mark):**

A $k$-mer is a contiguous subsequence of length $k$. Its normalized frequency is:

$$
f_{k\text{-mer}} = \frac{N_{k\text{-mer}}}{L - k + 1}
$$

The full feature vector concatenates frequencies of all $20^k$ possible k-mers. For $k=3$ (tri-peptides), $d = 8000$.

**4. PSSM Construction (2 marks):**

A PSSM $M \in \mathbb{R}^{L \times 20}$ is computed from a **multiple sequence alignment (MSA)**. For position $i$ and amino acid $a$:

$$
M_{i,a} = \log_2 \frac{p_{i,a}}{b_a}
$$

where $p_{i,a}$ is the observed frequency of $a$ at column $i$ in the MSA, and $b_a$ is the background frequency (often uniform $1/20$, or derived from a large sequence database such as UniRef). Positive $M_{i,a}$ indicates over-representation (likely conserved), negative indicates under-representation.
